title: The Secret to Marathon-Winning Humanoid Robots
blurb: The Honor Lightning humanoid ran a half-marathon in 50 minutes and 26 seconds on April 19, 2026, beating the human world record by 7 minutes and last year's best robot time by nearly two hours. An IEEE Spectrum analysis finds no exotic breakthrough behind the result, just careful drivetrain engineering, with optimal gearing around 45:1 keeping knee-motor heat near 150W and avoiding the overheating that forced rivals like Unitree to use ice backpacks.

title: Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment
blurb: OpenAI introduced Deployment Simulation, a method that replays past conversations in a privacy-preserving way against a candidate model to estimate how it will behave before release. Applied across GPT-5-series Thinking deployments, the technique improved estimates of undesired behavior rates, surfaced novel forms of misalignment, and reduced the chance that models could tell they were being tested.

title: Z.ai’s open-weights GLM-5.2 beats GPT-5.5 on multiple long-horizon coding benchmarks for 1/6th the cost
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, a 753B-parameter open-weights model under an unrestricted MIT license that beats GPT-5.5 on several long-horizon coding benchmarks at one-sixth the cost. Available now on Hugging Face, the Z.ai API, and 20-plus coding environments, it ships with a 1M-token context window and a new IndexShare architecture that cuts per-token compute by 2.9x at maximum length.

title: Qwen-Robot Suite: A Foundation Model Suite for Physical World Intelligence
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-Robot Suite, a set of foundation models aimed at physical-world intelligence for robotics. The suite targets perception, reasoning, and control so robots can interpret their surroundings and plan actions, extending Qwen's language and vision work into embodied systems.

title: Steam Workshop abused to spread malware via Wallpaper Engine app
blurb: Attackers abused Steam Workshop's Wallpaper Engine app to push DarkKomet backdoors, credential stealers, and cryptominers via malicious wallpaper packages.

title: Check out what's new in Android 17
blurb: Android 17 launched today, rolling out first to Pixel before reaching other eligible devices throughout 2026 with new productivity, gaming, and security features. Headline additions include Bubbles for floating any app into a compact window, Screen Reactions for combined selfie-and-screen recording, an optimized foldable gaming mode, and temporary precise-location and selective-contact sharing, with Gemini Intelligence coming to select advanced devices later this summer.

title: GLM-5.2 is the new leading open weights model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 is now the top open weights model on Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index, scoring 51 to lead MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek V4 Pro, both at 44. The MIT-licensed model keeps GLM-5.1's size and pricing while gaining most on scientific reasoning, expands its context window to 1M tokens, and lands on the intelligence-versus-cost Pareto frontier at roughly $0.46 per task.

title: Coinbase launching tokenized US stocks, backed 1:1, with holders able to receive dividends
blurb: Coinbase will launch tokenized US stocks backed 1:1 by real shares, with token holders eligible to receive dividends. The move puts Coinbase alongside rivals Robinhood and Kraken, as well as traditional financial institutions, in the race to bring equities trading on-chain.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD has quietly stripped Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME), which encrypts RAM contents to block physical attacks, from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, apparently after the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware update. The change is undetectable on Windows and hard to spot on Linux, and AMD has neither acknowledged whether it was intentional nor explained why a feature that worked on consumer chips for years now appears Pro-only.

title: Zama, Morpho and Steakhouse launch first ‘confidential DeFi yield’ vault on Ethereum
blurb: Zama, Morpho, and Steakhouse launched the first confidential DeFi yield vault on Ethereum, letting firms earn yield without exposing balances.

title: Public and Private Medical Community Targeted by China-Nexus Threat Actor Pursuing Artificial Intelligence, Cyber, Medical, and National Defense Research
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group disclosed a year-long campaign by UNC6508, a China-nexus actor that breached North American academic, medical, and military research institutions starting in September 2023. The group exploited public-facing REDCap servers, deployed custom INFINITERED malware, and exfiltrated data on AI, uncrewed vehicles, offensive cyber programs, and national defense before Google disrupted its infrastructure and notified affected organizations.

title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
blurb: Chrome and Firefox shipped updates patching over 70 vulnerabilities, including critical use-after-free bugs that could enable remote code execution.

title: Show HN: cuTile Rust: Safe, data-race-free GPU kernels in Rust
blurb: cuTile Rust is an Nvidia research project for writing memory-safe, data-race-free GPU kernels in idiomatic Rust, extending ownership across the GPU launch boundary by partitioning mutable tensors into disjoint pieces while sharing immutable ones. A `#[cutile::module]` macro captures each kernel's AST and JIT-compiles it through CUDA Tile IR into a cubin, supporting synchronous launches, async pipelines, and CUDA graph replay.

title: Joomla, LiteSpeed Vulnerabilities Exploited in Attacks
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting Joomla JCE (CVE-2026-48907) and LiteSpeed cPanel (CVE-2026-54420) flaws for code execution and root privilege escalation; patch immediately.

title: The State of Fable, The Jailbreak Problem, SpaceX Acquires Cursor
blurb: Stratechery's latest Update argues the administration is likely wrong in its assessment of Fable, but contends that managing the fallout is ultimately Anthropic's responsibility rather than the government's. The piece also covers the broader jailbreak problem facing frontier labs and SpaceX's acquisition of Cursor, tying model safety obligations to the companies that build and ship the systems.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and tested on Pixel 6a, 7, 7a, 8, and the 10 series, with the first official release due a day after Android 17's launch. The privacy-focused Android fork is shipping a final Android 16 QPR2 build alongside the new port, and public testing across all supported devices begins immediately.

title: Introducing tfctl: The CLI for HCP Terraform and TFE
blurb: HashiCorp released tfctl, the first official CLI for HCP Terraform and Terraform Enterprise platform operations like canceling runs, managing workspaces, and administering organizations.

title: Windows and Linux users: The deadline to update Secure Boot keys is near
blurb: Three Microsoft-signed certificates that anchor Secure Boot's chain of trust expire on June 24, requiring Windows and Linux users to update their cryptographic keys to keep verifying boot-time firmware and software. Without the update, systems lose protection against bootkits, firmware-level malware that loads before the OS and antivirus, survives reinstalls, and is notoriously hard to detect or remove.

title: Databricks sales growth tops 80%, but margin are shrinking from swarm of AI agents
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue jumped more than 80% from a year earlier to $6.9 billion, but CEO Ali Ghodsi told CNBC that gross margins are falling as AI agents generate far more queries and push up model costs. The consumption-based model now draws $1.7 billion a year from AI products, and at a $134 billion valuation Databricks is worth more than public rival Snowflake while staying off the IPO line.

title: AWS WAF adds AI traffic monetization capability to help content owners charge AI bots for content access
blurb: AWS WAF added an AI traffic monetization capability that lets publishers charge AI bots and agents per request for content access, set by path, bot category, or verification tier without changing origin infrastructure. The Bot Control feature collects payments in stablecoins through Coinbase's x402 Facilitator, with Stripe and Machine Payments Protocol support coming, as AI crawlers now exceed 50% of web traffic for many providers.

title: Macron Seeks Way Around Trump’s Ban on Anthropic’s AI Models
blurb: French President Emmanuel Macron will lead talks with G7 leaders and tech executives to explore deploying advanced AI models through "trusted partners," a workaround to US rules that block foreign nationals from accessing the technology. The effort follows Trump's ban on Anthropic's models abroad, and signals that export controls on frontier AI are becoming a flashpoint in transatlantic relations.

title: Intel begins production of most-advanced chip, inching closer to possible Apple deal
blurb: Intel began risk production of 18A-P, its most advanced chip node, which delivers 9% higher performance or 18% lower power than the 18A process it already runs at volume in Arizona. The milestone moves Intel closer to a possible deal to manufacture chips for Apple, though analysts say outside customers will hinge on Intel hitting yield rates above 90% in the first month.

title: RFC 10008: The new HTTP Query Method
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, defining a new HTTP QUERY method for sending safe, idempotent requests with a body, filling the gap between GET and POST. Unlike GET, it carries query input as request content rather than in the URI, and unlike POST, it is explicitly safe and idempotent, so requests can be automatically retried or cached without risk of partial state changes.

title: Java's Project Valhalla finally lands a preview in JDK 28
blurb: Java's Project Valhalla reached its first preview in JDK 28, introducing value classes that combine the performance of primitives with the abstraction of objects. Architect Brian Goetz cautioned that the feature will likely remain in preview through the next long-term support release, continuing a development effort that has run for over a decade.

title: Apple is about to make Hide My Email useless
blurb: Apple is moving all Sign in with Apple and Hide My Email aliases to a dedicated @private.icloud.com subdomain, making it trivial for services to identify and block relay addresses without affecting regular iCloud mailboxes. The change preserves the carve-out that gave the feature its value, since aliases were previously hard to ban without hitting legitimate iCloud users, and could push many sites to reject them outright like disposable email.

title: Coinbase to join tokenized stock race with onchain shares, dividend payments
blurb: Coinbase announced plans to launch tokenized stocks backed one-for-one by underlying U.S. equities, letting users own, trade, and redeem shares onchain while automatically collecting dividends. CEO Brian Armstrong framed the products as direct equity ownership rather than the derivatives or synthetic exposure of existing offerings, with the rollout starting in eligible jurisdictions outside the U.S. on an unannounced date.

title: Z.ai pitches GLM-5.2 for long-running software engineering tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-source coding model with a one million-token context window, claiming it trails Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.8 by 1% on the FrontierSWE long-horizon benchmark and edges out OpenAI's GPT-5.5 by the same margin. The model uses a technique called IndexShare to cut per-token compute by 2.9x at full context, targeting the cost of running coding agents across large repositories.

title: SubQ 1.1 Small
blurb: SubQ released the model card for SubQ 1.1 Small, a Subquadratic Sparse Attention model that hits near-perfect needle-in-a-haystack retrieval up to 12M tokens while cutting attention compute by nearly 1,000x. At 1M tokens it needs 64.5x less compute than dense attention and runs 56x faster than FlashAttention-2, with a broader lineup spanning 2M to 12M tokens planned for later this year.

title: Making ast.walk 220x Faster
blurb: Reflex rebuilt Python's `ast.walk` to run roughly 220x faster after the standard library version bottlenecked their AI code generator's custom linter. The speedup came from dropping generators and `yield` in the hot path, inlining `iter_child_nodes`, and replacing repeated suspend-resume overhead with a plain list that accumulates nodes during traversal.

title: Improvement in advanced Alzheimer’s disease following high-dose psilocybin
blurb: A case report describes cognitive and functional improvement in a patient with advanced Alzheimer's disease after treatment with high-dose psilocybin. The authors document gains in memory, mood, and daily functioning following the psychedelic compound, though a single uncontrolled case cannot establish efficacy and the findings will require controlled trials to validate.

title: Coinbase unveils ‘System Update’ unlocking an AI advisor, global unified liquidity, options trading and more
blurb: Coinbase's "System Update" adds an SEC-registered AI investment advisor, unified US-international liquidity, options trading, and pre-IPO perps.

title: Databricks unveils CustomerLake, its agentic CDP
blurb: Databricks announced CustomerLake, an agentic customer data platform, at its Data + AI Summit, marking its entry into the martech market. Built on the company's lakehouse architecture and governed by Unity Catalog, the product consolidates identity resolution, audience building, and campaign activation into one platform, deploying agents that analyze behavior and act on customer data in real time.

title: Building AI Agents for AR Glasses and XR Devices with NVIDIA XR AI
blurb: NVIDIA released XR AI in public beta, an open-source library for building AI agents that run on AR glasses and XR headsets by routing live camera and microphone streams to GPU-accelerated cloud or edge models. The agents combine visual grounding via Cosmos, voice interaction through Nemotron, and enterprise tool use over MCP, with early deployments at Siemens factories and Stanford and Princeton research labs.

title: Claude Corps
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Corps, a national fellowship that will train 1,000 fellows to use Claude, place them full-time at nonprofits for a year, and pay each an $85,000 salary plus benefits. The company committed an initial $150M to the program, which it runs with CodePath and Social Finance, framing it as direct investment in workers absorbing AI-driven economic disruption.

title: SpaceX alum nabs $22M to turn rocket engines into geothermal power plants
blurb: Critical Energy, founded by a SpaceX veteran who worked on Falcon Heavy and the Raptor engine, raised $22M to build modular geothermal turbines whose turbomachinery resembles rocket engines. The factory-built units target a turbine shortage slowing geothermal projects, with a first 2.5MW plant due by 2027 and CEO Spencer Jackson betting the sector reaches gigawatt scale before fission and fusion deploy commercially.

title: Astrobotic unveils Griffin-1 lunar lander
blurb: Astrobotic revealed Griffin-1, its second lunar lander, which it plans to launch in Q4 on a SpaceX Falcon Heavy after Peregrine failed to attempt a landing in 2024. Billed as the first infrastructure-class lunar lander, Griffin will carry 10 payloads from six nations, led by Astrolab's 500-kilogram FLEX rover, the heaviest commercial payload sent to the moon to date.

title: AI and brain-computer interface allow speechless ALS patient to work a full-time job
blurb: A UC Davis research team paired a brain-computer interface with machine learning to let a speechless ALS patient hold down a full-time job by decoding his intended speech in real time. The implant hardware itself is not new, but the team's decoding software translates neural signals into synthesized words fast enough for live conversation and work, restoring practical communication that the disease had taken away.

title: Intel’s performance-enhanced 18A-P process enters risk production — drop-in 18A upgrade promises 9% performance improvement at iso-power, cuts thermal resistance by 40%
blurb: Intel's 18A-P process, a performance-enhanced revision of its 18A node, has entered risk production, the low-volume stage before mass manufacturing. The node promises a 9% performance gain at the same power or an 18% power reduction at the same performance, plus 40% lower thermal resistance, and stays backward compatible so designers can port existing 18A chips like Panther Lake and Xeon 6+ without changes.

title: Copilot Cowork is now generally available
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide after a three-month Frontier preview, during which more than half of the Fortune 500 adopted it, including Accenture, Capital Group, Koch, and Zurich Insurance. Cowork runs complex, long-running, multi-tool tasks end-to-end in the cloud, and the GA release adds model choice, plugin extensibility, and usage-based billing with new cost controls.

title: Salesforce buys Fin for $3.6bn
blurb: Salesforce agreed to acquire Fin, the customer-service AI agent formerly known as Intercom, for $3.6B, with the deal expected to close in Q4 pending regulatory clearance. Fin's multichannel agent, which claims a 76% resolution rate across 12,000 customers, will fold into Salesforce's Agentforce platform, which Benioff says now runs at a $1.2B annual rate, up 205% year over year.

title: GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open model built for long-horizon coding agents with a 1M-token context and selectable thinking-effort levels. It ranks as the top open-source model on the FrontierSWE, PostTrainBench, and SWE-Marathon long-horizon benchmarks, trailing Anthropic's Opus 4.8 while edging out GPT-5.5 on FrontierSWE.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to its most powerful models after the company pulled its Fable and Mythos systems. Anthropic said both sides are working quickly to resolve the dispute, marking its first public comment since the removal and signaling the rising stakes of government oversight over frontier AI capabilities.

title: Brit competition cops order Google to make search rankings less mysterious
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority ordered Google to make its search ranking practices more transparent, requiring clearer disclosure of how results and prominence are determined. The order stems from Google's new "strategic market status" designation under the UK's digital markets regime, giving regulators standing power to impose conduct requirements on the company's search business.

title: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
blurb: OpenAI's net loss attributable to the company reached $38.53 billion in 2025, nearly 8 times its 2024 loss, on $13.07 billion in revenue against $34 billion in costs and expenses, according to audited financials verified by the Financial Times. The jump was driven partly by the company's conversion from a nonprofit to a for-profit, which added a $41.55 billion charge tied to changes in fair value of convertible interests and warrant liabilities.

title: Among the large new rockets Amazon was counting on, only Europe has delivered
blurb: Amazon has hundreds of completed Leo internet satellites sitting idle in Florida, with only Arianespace's Ariane 6 among its three new contracted rockets actually flying them to orbit. Blue Origin's New Glenn and ULA's Vulcan have yet to launch a single Leo satellite, leaving Amazon dependent on the soon-to-retire Atlas V and a French provider to build out its low-Earth-orbit constellation.

title: Apple releasing 20th anniversary iPhone, AirPods with cameras next year: report
blurb: Apple is preparing a 20th-anniversary iPhone with a nearly edge-to-edge curved display, a second-generation foldable, and camera-equipped AirPods for late 2027, according to a Bloomberg report from Mark Gurman. The anniversary models and foldable will run a 2-nanometer A21 chip, while the AirPods, delayed from 2026 by Apple's AI and Siri struggles, will use visual models to identify objects around the user.

title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
blurb: Anthropic suspended access to its latest model, Fable 5, three days after launch and restricted its Mythos 5 models, complying with a US national-security order barring foreign nationals, including its own employees, from using the technology. The directive followed Anthropic research showing adversaries increasingly use frontier models to write malicious code, find vulnerabilities, and automate attack chains, with UK government testing confirming the models can run end-to-end intrusions.

title: Nvidia reveals AI robots that taught themselves to install GPUs into motherboards — video shows robot ‘solve high-precision tasks like… installing GPUs all by itself’
blurb: Nvidia showcased ENPIRE, an open-source framework that gave eight Codex agents a fleet of robots, GPUs, and a token budget, then let them teach themselves high-precision tasks like slotting a graphics card into a motherboard, sorting metal pins, and cutting zip ties. The robots practiced on real hardware, read papers, and refined their own control code to fix failures, demonstrating autonomous research in the physical world.

title: AMD acquires MEXT to add predictive memory optimization to its AI stack
blurb: AMD acquired memory optimization startup MEXT to fold predictive memory software into its AI infrastructure stack, with financial terms undisclosed. MEXT's technology uses AI to move frequently accessed data between flash and DRAM, extending effective memory capacity as enterprises grapple with DRAM prices Gartner expects to rise sharply through 2026.

title: Mobileye is entering the US robotaxi market with standalone service
blurb: Mobileye will launch a vertically integrated US robotaxi service in an unnamed city in 2027, using its Moovit platform to handle bookings and fleet coordination. The Israeli firm, spun out of Intel in 2022, plans to start with around 100 vehicles early next year as it shifts from supplying autonomous-driving tech to owning the operation.

title: The US government awards $500M under the CHIPS Act to SandboxAQ to use AI to develop new chemicals and materials for domestic semiconductor manufacturing (Stephen Nellis/Reuters)
blurb: The US government awarded $500M to SandboxAQ under the CHIPS Act to use AI for designing new chemicals and materials for domestic semiconductor manufacturing. The grant ties the Alphabet spinout's AI-simulation work to US chip self-sufficiency, funding research into the specialty materials that fabs depend on but the country largely imports.

title: Salesforce to buy AI customer service platform Fin for $3.6 billion to boost agentic offerings
blurb: Salesforce will buy AI customer service platform Fin, formerly Intercom, for about $3.6B to expand its agentic AI lineup. The deal complements Agentforce by adding Fin's AI agent, which resolves chat, email, WhatsApp, text, phone, and Slack queries, and lands as Salesforce fends off fears that AI tools could make its SaaS model obsolete amid a stock down over a third in 2026.

title: First official details of AMD's next-gen 'Mustang Peak' Threadripper CPUs come into view — chips feature DDR5, PCIe 6.0, and a new socket
blurb: AMD's next-generation Threadripper CPUs, code-named Mustang Peak, will use Zen 6 cores built on a 2nm-class TSMC process, with DDR5 memory, PCIe 6.0, and a new TR6 platform. The leaked technical documentation points to up to 144 cores on Threadripper Pro parts, up from 96, with clock rates targeting well above 6 GHz.

title: Google launches 'bubble' multi-tasking and foldable gaming mode with Android 17
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to Pixel devices today, adding Bubbles for floating multi-app windows, a 50/50 foldable gaming mode, and granular location and contact permissions. Gemini Intelligence, Google's context-aware system for multi-step tasks, arrives on select advanced devices this summer, while the OS reaches other Android phones throughout the year.

title: Bing Webmaster Tools updates AI reporting with Intents, Topics, Citation Share and Compare
blurb: Bing Webmaster Tools added Intents, Topics, Citation Share, and Compare to its AI performance report, now rolling out in global preview.

title: Qualcomm mulls taking over Jim Keller's Tenstorrent, report claims — deal for AI chipmaker would value the company at between $8 billion and $10 billion
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to acquire Jim Keller's AI chipmaker Tenstorrent at a valuation between $8B and $10B, according to The Information. The deal would be one of Qualcomm's largest ever and is driven by interest in Tenstorrent's RISC-V AI accelerators and data center CPU IP, though it would add to an already crowded lineup that includes the company's Hexagon-based AI200 and AI250 accelerators and recently acquired Ventana Micro.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: The curl project will pause all vulnerability report handling during July 2026 to give its maintainers a rest after months of submission pressure.

title: Control whether your users can have temporary chats and delete conversations in the Gemini app
blurb: Google Workspace admins can now control whether users get temporary, unsaved Gemini chats and the ability to delete conversation history, on by default.

title: France's digital sovereignty push is struggling to escape the Microsoft gravity well
blurb: France's drive for digital sovereignty remains heavily dependent on Microsoft, with government agencies and public institutions still routing core systems through the company's software and cloud services. The report details how procurement inertia, entrenched integrations, and a lack of mature local alternatives keep European-built options on the margins, undercutting the political goal of reducing reliance on US tech providers.

title: Leaked financial docs show OpenAI is losing billions of dollars a year
blurb: Leaked audited financials obtained by journalist Ed Zitron show OpenAI's revenue grew from $3.7B in 2024 to $13.07B in 2025, while its loss from operations widened from $8.78B to $20.92B over the same period. R&D alone hit $19.18B in 2025, including $10.59B paid to Microsoft, complicating the company's pitch to investors that it can reach profitability by 2030 ahead of an expected IPO.

title: Microsoft might put China’s DeepSeek inside Copilot to tame its AI bills
blurb: Microsoft is exploring a self-hosted, fine-tuned version of China's DeepSeek V4 to power Copilot Cowork, its agentic Microsoft 365 assistant, as a cheaper alternative to the Anthropic and OpenAI models it runs today. The move pairs with a shift to usage-based pricing for Cowork and reflects rising agentic-AI compute costs, though any DeepSeek option would be optional, Azure-hosted, and politically fraught amid Washington's scrutiny of Chinese AI.

title: Anthropic to meet with Trump administration over Mythos dispute
blurb: Anthropic will meet with Trump administration officials in Washington on Monday to resolve a federal directive that forced it to suspend access to its new Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The Friday order cited an unspecified national security threat and barred use by any foreign national, deepening a rift that already saw the Pentagon label Anthropic a supply chain risk in March.

title: New expanded Android parental controls to support families around the world
blurb: Google is expanding its Android Parental Controls to all devices updating to Android 17, bringing screen-time limits, downtime schedules, Google Play content filters, and per-app blocking into a single PIN-protected settings hub previously available only on Pixel. The company also raised its U.S. digital wellbeing fund to more than $50M to back interventions on healthier technology use and combating social isolation among kids and teens.

title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
blurb: The Justice Department asked a federal court to halt a citizen pollution lawsuit against xAI's data center in Memphis, citing national security and Elon Musk's role in the recent Iran war. The DOJ also argued it has the authority to block environmental suits brought by citizens, a claim that could reshape how communities challenge industrial facilities under federal law.

title: Estonia says it will assign personal ID numbers to AI agents to give them "limited, controllable, and auditable authorizations" as they take actions for humans (Ott Tammik/Bloomberg)
blurb: Estonia said it will issue personal ID numbers to AI agents, giving each one "limited, controllable, and auditable authorizations" when it acts on a person's behalf. The plan extends the country's digital-identity system to autonomous software, creating a legal framework for tracing and constraining agent actions as they begin handling tasks for citizens and businesses.

title: Chinese memory brands ditch Samsung and Micron for homegrown CXMT and YMTC silicon — Corsair, HP, and Dell are already adopting the China-produced DDR5 chips
blurb: Chinese memory brands are dropping Samsung, Micron, and SK Hynix in favor of homegrown DDR5 from CXMT and NAND from YMTC, both heavily subsidized by Beijing to prioritize domestic supply over data-center margins. The shift is already reaching Western vendors, with Corsair shipping CXMT chips in some Vengeance kits and HP and Dell qualifying the China-produced silicon for their own systems.

title: AMD takes over MEXT for memory tiering tech that enables flash to appear as DRAM to applications — tech to 'address growing memory constraints' in the data center
blurb: AMD acquired MEXT, a startup whose AI-based memory tiering makes NAND flash appear as DRAM to applications, moving infrequently accessed data to cheaper storage while predicting which pages to pull back before they're needed. AMD plans to fold MEXT's Predictive Memory Engine into its data center portfolio to cut DRAM costs and ease the memory bottlenecks increasingly throttling large AI workloads.

title: Apple collects every tap to deliver App Store personalized recommendations
blurb: Apple's new App Store Personalized Collections feature logs every tap users make to power its tailored app recommendations, and security researchers at Mysk found the analytics capture extends to identifiable data like typing speed during searches. There is no toggle to disable the tracking, which sits at odds with Apple's privacy-focused marketing and its usual practice of offering opt-out controls for data collection.

title: Qualcomm reveals flagship XR processor and new framework for AI glasses
blurb: Qualcomm announced Snapdragon Reality Elite, a flagship XR platform that replaces the XR2+ Gen 2 with 60% faster graphics, a 30% CPU uplift, 48 TOPS of NPU performance, and support for 4.4K-per-eye displays at 90fps. Alongside it, the company introduced Snapdragon START, a turnkey toolkit of hardware modules, software, and white-label designs meant to speed smart-glasses development, with XREAL's Project Aura the first device on the new chip.

title: Even more batteries included with Emacs
blurb: The third installment of a series surfacing useful, lesser-known features built into stock Emacs, like dictionary-tooltip-mode.

title: Intel starts cooking up enhanced 18A-P silicon for would-be foundry customers
blurb: Intel began producing 18A-P, an enhanced version of its 18A process that adds higher performance and clock speeds, to court external foundry customers. The node is a refinement of the 18A technology already used in Intel's own Panther Lake chips, and serves as a bridge to the 14A process Intel is pitching to win contract manufacturing deals against TSMC and Samsung.

title: DocLang aims to make documents readable by AI, not humans
blurb: IBM, Nvidia, and Red Hat have formed a working group, hosted by the Linux Foundation's LF AI & Data project, to build DocLang, an open document format designed for AI systems rather than human readers. The spec defines a structured, machine-readable format built for LLM tokenizers, extending the DocLing toolkit so enterprises can prepare and govern document data more reliably than they can with PDFs and other human-oriented formats.

title: Fox Buys Roku, The Problem With Fox’s Smart Strategy, Streaming That Works
blurb: Fox acquired Roku, betting that owning a streaming distribution platform is worth more than extracting maximum fees from rights holders. The market sold off on the deal, but the analysis argues Fox is trading its position as a content extractor for leverage as a "renter" that controls the pipe to viewers, a bet that streaming distribution now matters more than content negotiation.

title: Mobileye’s US robotaxi launch will put it on both sides of the AV business
blurb: Mobileye said it will launch a robotaxi service in an unnamed US city in 2027, starting with 100 autonomous vehicles and aiming to scale to roughly 17,000 within five years. The move makes the Intel subsidiary both an AV technology supplier and an operator, putting it in direct competition with customers like Volkswagen that already license its self-driving system.

title: Microsoft faces down sueball, capacity problems in series of challenges
blurb: Microsoft is contending with a shareholder lawsuit alleging it made misleading statements about Copilot and AI adoption, alongside mounting datacenter capacity constraints. The combination highlights the gap between Microsoft's aggressive AI marketing and the infrastructure and revenue reality, as investors press for proof that Copilot demand justifies the company's spending.

title: Uber to Offer Robotaxis in Houston With Lucid and Nuro, Posing Challenge to Waymo
blurb: Uber will launch driverless robotaxi rides in Houston using vehicles built by Lucid and Nuro, expanding its autonomous footprint into the fourth-largest US city. The move sets up a direct challenge to Waymo, which has led commercial robotaxi deployment, and reinforces Uber's strategy of partnering with carmakers and AV developers rather than building self-driving tech in-house.

title: Twenty, which uses AI to help US military hackers penetrate adversary computer networks, raised a $100M Series B led by Accel at a $1B valuation (Colin Demarest/Axios)
blurb: Twenty raised a $100M Series B led by Accel at a $1B valuation to expand its AI tools that help US military hackers penetrate adversary computer networks. The round reflects rising investor appetite for offensive cyber and defense-tech startups, as the Pentagon pushes to automate operations once handled manually by human operators.

title: SMI says Nvidia is driving its consumer PCIe 6.0 roadmap, not AMD and Intel — RTX Spark agentic AI platform fuels a hunger for storage bandwidth
blurb: Silicon Motion will align its consumer PCIe 6.0 SSD controller roadmap with Nvidia rather than AMD or Intel, with a client-grade Gen6 platform targeted for late 2027. The company credits Nvidia's data-hungry client chips like GB10 and future RTX Spark for driving the timeline, since AMD and Intel are in no rush to adopt the costly interface and only enterprise Gen6 drives ship this year.

title: SpaceX stock jumps 20% in first full day of trading after record debut
blurb: SpaceX stock rose 20% on its first full trading day, closing at $192.50 after the largest IPO in history valued the company above $2 trillion. The rally has sparked debate over the valuation, with CFRA initiating a sell rating at a $115 target and Morningstar pegging fair value at $63, against 2025 revenue of $18.7 billion and a near-$5 billion loss.

title: Pinterest launches an experimental AI shopping app called ‘Ask Pinterest’
blurb: Pinterest launched Ask Pinterest, an experimental standalone app that turns its visual discovery into a conversational, chatbot-style interface for shopping and product recommendations. The app draws on Pinterest's Taste Graph and users' saved Pins to handle multi-step queries like planning a dinner party, and arrives alongside a new Model Context Protocol for advertisers as AI chatbots increasingly compete with search for shoppers.

title: Google’s Gemini-Powered AI Home Speaker Goes on Sale June 25
blurb: Google will release its $100 Google Home Speaker on June 25, a voice device built around deep integration with the company's Gemini AI platform. The launch puts Gemini directly into living-room hardware, pitting Google against Amazon's Alexa and Apple's HomePod as assistants shift from scripted commands to conversational models.

title: AI note-taking is now available in Google Voice
blurb: Google Voice now takes AI notes and transcribes calls, accessible only to whoever started the capture and gated behind admin consent settings.

title: Anthropic asked for regulation. Washington went much further
blurb: The Trump administration ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its newest Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for any foreign national, citing unspecified national security authorities. The directive lands days after CEO Dario Amodei called for binding AI regulation that lets the government block unsafe models, and Anthropic, now valued near $1 trillion, disputes the recall as a "misunderstanding" it is racing to resolve.

title: Blue Origin Says It Will Launch a New Glenn Rocket This Year
blurb: Blue Origin said it will launch its New Glenn rocket again this year, and is already rebuilding the Florida launch site where the vehicle exploded last month. The fast turnaround keeps alive the company's push to challenge SpaceX, which has dominated the heavy-lift market while Blue Origin works to prove New Glenn can fly reliably.

title: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first debt sale since start of AI boom
blurb: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first bond sale since the AI boom began, with sources saying the total could approach $25 billion. The move follows similar capital raises from Alphabet, Amazon, and Super Micro, and comes as Nvidia funds an $80 billion buyback program after generating $49 billion in free cash flow last quarter.

title: Databricks Releases General AI Agents for Businesses
blurb: Databricks released general-purpose AI agents that handle open-ended business work like research, analysis, and report generation across a company's data. The launch pushes the data-analytics firm beyond its infrastructure roots into the AI applications layer, where it competes with OpenAI, Microsoft, and a crowded field of enterprise agent startups.

title: Exclusive: Mindbeam touts dramatic performance improvements in CPU-based AI inference
blurb: Mindbeam released Litespark-Inference, an open-source framework that runs ternary LLMs on standard Apple, Intel, AMD, and Arm CPUs with 17x to 96x higher throughput and over 80% lower memory use than standard PyTorch. The approach constrains model weights to -1, 0, and +1 to cut multiplication overhead, positioning CPUs as complements to scarce GPUs for cost-sensitive, memory-constrained edge inference.

title: Self-driving tech supplier Mobileye targets U.S. robotaxi launch in 2027
blurb: Mobileye said it will launch its own U.S. robotaxi service in 2027, deploying an initial 100-vehicle fleet in a major city and scaling to roughly 17,000 vehicles over five years. The move puts the Jerusalem-based supplier in direct competition with automakers that use its Mobileye Drive technology, entering a market led by Waymo, which now operates across 11 U.S. cities.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: Ahrefs analyzed 137K domains and found 97% of published llms.txt files got zero traffic in May 2026, mostly bots when fetched.

title: Light-driven memory breakthrough targets AI power consumption
blurb: Researchers at Japan's QST demonstrated a magnetic memory material that rewrites with ultrashort laser pulses instead of electric current, using a CoFeB artificial ferrimagnet switched by a single femtosecond pulse. Built on a material already common in spintronic memory and compatible with magnetic tunnel junctions, the approach could cut the heat and energy losses of electrically driven memory while enabling faster write speeds for AI data centers.

title: Hetzner Price Adjustment
blurb: Hetzner raised prices on new dedicated and cloud server orders and rescales starting June 15, 2026.

title: Anthropic suspends Fable and Mythos systems "for all users" after US government claims jailbreak risk
blurb: Anthropic suspended its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all users days after release, complying with Department of Commerce export controls imposed by the Trump administration over an alleged jailbreak vulnerability. The company disputed the reasoning, arguing the "narrow, non-universal jailbreak" exists in rival models like OpenAI's GPT-5.5 and that the standard, if applied industry-wide, would halt all frontier deployments.

title: KDE Plasma 6.7 Released
blurb: KDE Plasma 6.7 shipped with per-screen virtual desktops, a feature requested for 21 years, alongside a quick light/dark Global Theme toggle and System Tray support for Flatpak "Background Apps." The release also adds per-app volume controls, easier shared-printer setup on Windows networks with a new print queue management tool, and Overview-screen desktop switching via scroll or Page Up/Down.

title: DOJ claims xAI’s unpermitted gas turbines are a matter of ‘national, economic, and energy security’
blurb: The DOJ sided with xAI in a lawsuit brought by the NAACP that seeks to shut down dozens of unpermitted natural gas turbines powering Elon Musk's data centers near Memphis. The Justice Department argued that Grok supports "mission-critical" military operations, so cutting the power would threaten national, economic, and energy security, even as the SpaceX division plans to buy another $2.8B in turbines over three years.

title: Google develops Personalization controls for Gemini
blurb: Google is building hidden Gemini controls that prioritize paid subscriptions and let users inspect and edit context on a per-app basis.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Limits on Its $200-a-Month AI Plans
blurb: A proposed class-action lawsuit accuses Anthropic of overselling usage allowances on its $200-a-month Claude subscription tiers, alleging customers paid for capacity the company did not deliver. The suit, which is seeking class-action status, centers on limits Anthropic placed on its priciest plans, adding legal pressure as AI providers tighten usage caps to manage soaring inference costs.

title: SpaceX IPO raises total of $85.7 billion as underwriters exercise 'greenshoe' overallotment option
blurb: SpaceX raised a total of $85.7 billion in its IPO after underwriters exercised the greenshoe overallotment option, adding to the initial $75 billion raised Thursday. The sale makes Elon Musk's space and AI company one of the largest public offerings on record, giving it deep capital to fund Starship development and its growing AI ambitions.

title: Salesforce acquires Fin, formerly known as Intercom
blurb: Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire Fin, the customer-support agent vendor that rebranded from Intercom a month ago, for roughly $3.6B. The deal is Salesforce's fifth acquisition of 2026 and folds Fin's Apex support model into Agentforce, which hit $1.2B in ARR last quarter, up 20% year over year.

title: Xreal unveils Aura, its lightweight smart glasses powered by Android XR
blurb: Xreal released Aura, lightweight see-through XR glasses running Google's Android XR and Qualcomm's new Snapdragon Reality Elite chip, launching this Fall. Weighing under 95 grams, Aura offers a 70-degree field of view, six-degrees-of-freedom hand tracking, and deep Gemini integration, with access to Google Play apps that float as windows or anchor to real-world surfaces.

title: Tianwen-2 makes series of burns on approach to asteroid, according to radio tracking
blurb: China's Tianwen-2 spacecraft completed a series of small propulsive burns after its June 7 main maneuver, setting up a July rendezvous with near-Earth asteroid 469219 Kamoʻoalewa. The probe will map the 40-100-meter quasi-moon and collect samples using three redundant techniques, then return them to Earth before flying on to main-belt comet 311P, arriving in 2035.

title: Boot Naked Linux
blurb: A developer strips a Linux system down to just the kernel and a custom C init to achieve near-instant boot times.

title: Payments startup Flutterwave hits $3.2B valuation, backed by Ripple
blurb: Flutterwave raised a Series E that values the African payments company at $3.2B, with Ripple taking an equity stake to jointly expand financial services across the continent. The undisclosed round pushes Flutterwave's total funding past $500M and gives it Ripple's infrastructure to grow digital asset offerings across the 35 African countries where it operates.

title: Qualcomm unveils its Snapdragon Reality Elite chip for next-gen AR headsets
blurb: Qualcomm released the Snapdragon Reality Elite, a new top-tier XR chip that supports 4.4K-per-eye resolution at 90 fps while running 12C cooler and extending battery life up to 20% over the XR2+ Gen 2. It delivers 60% higher GPU, 30% higher CPU, and 160% higher NPU performance, enabling smaller see-through headsets with on-device AI features like photorealistic avatars, starting with Xreal's Aura glasses.

title: US battery manufacturing output continues to break records
blurb: US battery manufacturing output hit another record high in early 2026, according to Federal Reserve industrial production data tracking the sector. The sustained climb reflects a wave of domestic gigafactory buildouts driven by EV demand and IRA manufacturing incentives, marking a steep departure from years of flat output before 2021.

title: Google releases Android 17 for Pixel phones
blurb: Google released the stable build of Android 17, rolling out today to Pixel devices from the Pixel 6 lineup through the Pixel 10 series. The update adds Bubbles floating-app windows, Screen Reactions for recording your face alongside the screen, tighter permission and anti-theft controls, and gaming optimizations, while Gemini Intelligence ships later this summer to select advanced devices.

title: Meet the new Google Home Speaker, built for Gemini
blurb: Google released the Google Home Speaker, its first audio device built for the Gemini for Home assistant, available for pre-order now and shipping June 25 for $99.99. The speaker swaps rigid command phrases for natural language, supports multi-step requests and follow-up questions, ships 10 new voices, and gates extra features like Gemini Live behind a Google Home Premium subscription.

title: Google Open-Sources a Knowledge Format and Wires It Into Its Catalog
blurb: Google Cloud published the Open Knowledge Format on June 12, a 451-line spec that represents AI-agent knowledge as a directory of plain markdown files requiring just one field, and the same day updated its Knowledge Catalog to ingest and serve that format to agents. By open-sourcing the portable format while keeping storage, serving, and access control in its paid catalog, Google commoditizes the knowledge layer and routes demand toward the products it still sells.

title: Wear OS 7 arrives to Pixel watches with Gemini AI and live updates
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7 for the Pixel Watch 2, 3, and 4, adding Gemini-powered widget creation, Live Updates for game scores and deliveries, and control over earbuds and upcoming Android XR smartglasses. The update also delivers roughly 10% better battery life, though Google has not said when it will reach Samsung Galaxy Watches or other Wear OS devices.

title: Microsoft announces 12th-gen Surface Pro with Snapdragon X2 processors
blurb: Microsoft released the 12th-gen Surface Pro, swapping the Snapdragon X1 for Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2 chips that deliver up to 53% faster graphics, an 80 TOPS NPU, and 15.5 hours of battery life. The base configuration now starts at $1,499, up from $999 last generation, with RAM options extending to 64GB and a free keyboard promo running through June 30.

title: Game Engine White Papers: Commander Keen
blurb: The Game Engine White Papers: Commander Keen is a 214-page, full-color book detailing how the 1990 platformer was built on late-eighties hardware, covering the 80286 CPU, EGA video cards, sound cards, game assets, and the engine itself. It is available as a paid paperback on Amazon or a free high-res PDF, with the book's source code published on GitHub.

title: China pushes for AI safety as G7 summit wraps up without Beijing
blurb: Chinese officials said Beijing is accelerating plans for a global AI cooperation organization and pushing cheap, openly downloadable models to the Global South, just as the G7 discussed restricting top US models to "trusted partners." The split reflects two competing strategies: the US gating subscription-only frontier models behind alliances, while China courts developing economies through BRICS, the UN, and free access.

title: Carvana is expanding into new vehicles. The implications could reshape the U.S. automotive retail market
blurb: Carvana has quietly bought seven new-vehicle franchises since last year, mostly selling Stellantis' Chrysler, Dodge, Jeep, and Ram brands, marking its first move beyond used cars. Its Casa Grande, Arizona store sold over 700 new vehicles last month to become Stellantis' top-volume location nationally, a jump from 30 to 50 monthly before the acquisition that analysts say could disrupt the $1.3T franchised dealer system.

title: Microsoft unveils new Surface Laptop with improved trackpad, Snapdragon X2, and more
blurb: Microsoft announced the eighth-gen Surface Laptop with Snapdragon X2 chips, a haptic trackpad, 80 TOPS NPUs, and a $1,599 starting price.

title: Uber will bring its premium robotaxi service to Houston in 2027
blurb: Uber will launch its premium robotaxi service in Houston by mid-2027, its second US market under a partnership with EV maker Lucid and self-driving startup Nuro. Uber will own and operate the Lucid Gravity fleet, putting it in direct competition with Alphabet's Waymo, which already runs commercial robotaxis in both Houston and San Francisco.

title: China's supreme court bans Infineon from selling GaN power chips in China — market-leader Innoscience secures major victory in multi-region patent war
blurb: China's Supreme People's Court upheld an injunction barring Infineon from selling the disputed gallium nitride power chips in mainland China, the final ruling in a patent case brought by market leader Innoscience. Both companies sit on Nvidia's approved supplier list for 800V AI-rack power delivery, and the multi-region war has split decisions across courts, with a separate US ITC ruling and German cases favoring Infineon.

title: DeepL acquires Mixhalo for live-event audio streaming and translation
blurb: DeepL acquired Mixhalo, a real-time audio startup that streams live-event sound directly to attendees' devices, to extend its translation suite into conferences and venues. Mixhalo, founded in 2016 and already a DeepL customer, raised over $39M from investors including Founders Fund and Cowboy Ventures, and gives DeepL a live-event channel for the voice-to-voice translation it launched in April.

title: NASA said nyet to Roscosmos plan to cut into leaky ISS segment
blurb: NASA rejected a Roscosmos proposal to cut into the leak-prone PrK transfer tunnel on the ISS Zvezda module, citing concerns the work could worsen the persistent air leaks rather than fix them. The crack-riddled segment has lost pressure for years, and the two agencies remain at odds over the cause as the station nears its planned 2030 retirement.

title: Thread Direct looks to solve Matter’s biggest setup headache
blurb: Thread Direct is a new onboarding feature for the Thread smart home protocol that lets phones with built-in Thread radios set up devices like smart plugs and locks without a separate border router. The change targets a long-standing Matter setup barrier, since many homes lack always-on hubs like Apple TVs or Nest Hubs, and uses radios already present in recent iPhones, Pixels, and Samsung flagships.

title: Trump admin abandons fight against wind energy as clean energy output surges
blurb: The Trump administration dropped its appeal defending a January 2025 executive order that froze federal permitting and leasing for wind projects, after a district judge ruled the ban unlawful and "arbitrary and capricious." The retreat clears a major obstacle for the energy transition as a record 79.7 GW of clean power is projected to come online in the US in 2026.

title: Poland Invests $11 Million in ElevenLabs to Build AI Tech Hub
blurb: Poland acquired a stake in voice AI company ElevenLabs for $11M, part of a bid to position itself as a major European AI hub. The investment ties the government to one of Europe's most prominent AI startups, founded by Polish entrepreneurs, as the country competes with France and Germany for regional AI leadership.

title: Pentagon boasts of using AI to write reports mandated by Congress
blurb: Pentagon CTO Emil Michael told the Hudson Institute that the Department of Defense now uses generative AI to draft its hundreds of congressionally mandated annual reports, cutting one task from 200 hours of staffing to five. The tools run on GenAI.mil, the department's internal platform launched in December 2025 starting with Google Cloud's Gemini for Government, now available across all six military branches.

title: How Millions of Digital Home Devices Are Secretly Powering Cyberattacks
blurb: Cheap consumer gadgets like routers, cameras, and smart-home devices are being quietly conscripted into proxy networks that route some of the most damaging cyberattacks on record. A WSJ investigation traces how foreign companies hijack these devices, masking attack traffic behind ordinary home IP addresses and leaving owners unaware their hardware is part of the operation.

title: Android 17 is about to make gaming on foldables way better
blurb: Android 17 introduces a foldable gaming mode that splits the screen 50/50, placing the game on the top half and a dynamic gamepad on the bottom to keep controls within reach without blocking the action. Google is also adding native controller remapping and improved memory cleanup to reduce frame drops, with the foldable features rolling out in the coming months.

title: Judge Dismisses Elon Musk’s xAI Trade-Secret Lawsuit Against OpenAI
blurb: A federal judge dismissed Elon Musk's xAI lawsuit accusing OpenAI of stealing trade secrets by hiring away an xAI engineer who had worked on the company's chatbot. The ruling clears OpenAI of the poaching claim, marking another setback in Musk's broader legal campaign against the company he co-founded and now competes with through xAI.

title: Matter 1.6 and Product Security 1.1 officially announced, here’s what’s new
blurb: The Connectivity Standards Alliance announced Matter 1.6, a feature release that lets the full device commissioning exchange run over NFC so a smart bulb or in-wall switch can be set up before it has power. The update also adds Joint Fabric for shared multi-ecosystem network administration and standardized thermostat suggestions, alongside a companion Product Security 1.1 specification.

title: U.S. gov't asks court to dismiss NAACP lawsuit against Elon Musk's xAI over use of unpermitted gas turbines — DOJ says Grok model running at Colossus 2 ‘supports mission-critical operations’
blurb: The DOJ filed a memorandum backing the dismissal of an NAACP lawsuit that targets xAI's use of unpermitted natural gas turbines at its Colossus 2 data center, arguing that cutting power would threaten national, economic, and energy security. The government said Grok is one of only four AI models cleared to support military operations on Secret and Top-Secret networks, elevating a local environmental permitting fight into a national security dispute.

title: SpaceX purchases Cursor, a Claude Code and OpenAI Codex competitor [U]
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in a roughly $60B deal, a week after the rocket company went public and following an earlier $10B partnership struck in April. The purchase strengthens a direct competitor to Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, giving Cursor access to xAI's Colossus infrastructure to scale training of its agentic coding models.

title: Marvell details vision of optically-interconnected data centers spanning across thousands of kilometers — new interconnects sampling later this year would allow CSPs to pool resources based on workload
blurb: At Computex 2026, Marvell CEO Matt Murphy detailed a plan for optical interconnects that pool compute and memory across data centers separated by thousands of kilometers, dissolving the campus boundaries set by copper's latency limits. The company's Colorz 1600 1.6 Tb/s coherent optical solution, built on a 2nm DSP for inter-data-center links, samples later this year, letting cloud providers allocate resources dynamically by workload.

title: DARPA seeks swappable satellites to help with future star wars
blurb: DARPA issued a call for modular, swappable satellites designed to be reconfigured or upgraded in orbit, aimed at supporting future space-based defense systems. The agency wants spacecraft built from interchangeable components so payloads can be replaced without launching entirely new satellites, a shift toward treating orbital assets as serviceable hardware rather than single-mission vehicles.

title: AI search grounded in Facebook posts? What could go wrong?
blurb: Meta added AI Mode to search in the Facebook app, a tool for complex queries like trip planning that draws on public posts across Facebook Groups and Instagram Reels. The data pool is a liability: early testing surfaced influencer spam and an AI-generated map with misplaced geography, plus a dodgy answer framing January 6 rioters as patriots, though it largely resisted prompts for outright misinformation.

title: Satya Nadella warns that AI could hollow out entire industries, echoing the damage done by globalization
blurb: Satya Nadella's essay warns that AI could centralize value in a few frontier models and hollow out entire industries.

title: Qualcomm takes spatial computing into the AI era with Snapdragon Reality Elite
blurb: Qualcomm released Snapdragon Reality Elite, a rebrand of its XR2+ Gen 2 chip that delivers up to 48 TOPS for on-device AI, enough to run 3B-parameter Llama models locally for translation and summarization. The renaming reflects Qualcomm's push to reposition its XR silicon as an edge-AI platform, betting that smarter on-device inference will drive the next wave of smart glasses and mixed-reality wearables.

title: China conducts 4 launches in 3 days, but silence follows Kuaizhou–11 launch
blurb: China carried out four orbital launches in three days, including a Kuaizhou-11 solid rocket that fell silent for more than eight hours after liftoff from Jiuquan, a pattern that usually signals a launch failure or payload trouble. The successful missions added nine more Guowang broadband satellites via a Long March 12, bringing that 13,000-satellite constellation to 177 in orbit, and sent the experimental Shijian-31 toward geostationary orbit.

title: Snap Inc. launches new SPECS augmented reality glasses (for 'just' $2,195)
blurb: Snap Inc. released SPECS, standalone augmented reality glasses that run on two Snapdragon processors and start at $2,195. The tetherless design uses an LCOS display with a 51-degree field of view, delivers 7ms motion-to-photon latency, and runs about four hours on a charge or 20 hours of mixed use with the included case.

title: Google prepares Personalization and AI Editing for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is preparing two NotebookLM features, Personal Intelligence and AI Editing, both surfacing in testing ahead of a likely rollout. Personal Intelligence gives the tool memory of past conversations, limited to activity inside NotebookLM rather than Gmail or Docs, while AI Editing lets users push saved note text back into chat to rework it, closing a gap where responses couldn't be changed once created.

title: iOS 27 basically turns your iPhone and AirPods into an Apple Watch at the gym
blurb: With iOS 27, Apple is extending GymKit to iPhone and AirPods Pro 3, letting users tap compatible gym equipment to track workouts without an Apple Watch. AirPods Pro 3 supply heart-rate data while the machine sends back incline, pace, distance, and calories to the Fitness app, positioning the feature as a backup for watch owners and a sampler that could draw new Apple Watch buyers.

title: MSI MPG Coreliquid P22 360 Review: Low noise, strong performance, budget price
blurb: MSI's MPG Coreliquid P22 360 delivers strong thermal performance, low noise, and a 2.1-inch IPS display for $129.99.

title: Enthusiast hacks Valve’s AMD-first gaming OS to run on Intel hardware — SteamOS boots on Intel Arc B580 desktop GPU, but it takes a Radeon card, installer workaround, and Resizable BAR fix
blurb: An enthusiast got Valve's AMD-first SteamOS booting on an Intel Arc B580, but only via a Radeon card and installer workarounds.

title: I admire Fabrice Bellard. He is almost certainly a better overall programmer
blurb: Carmack praises Fabrice Bellard, the FFmpeg and QEMU creator whose code quietly powers most internet video and virtualization.

title: Show HN: High-Res Neural Cellular Automata
blurb: Neural cellular automata pair a coarse-grid NCA with a lightweight implicit decoder to render self-organizing patterns at arbitrary resolution in real time.

title: VAST Data and the evolving neoclouds
blurb: VAST Data has become the dominant storage supplier to neoclouds, landing a 600 PB capacity win at Australia's Sharon AI that cements its lead in GPU-focused AI infrastructure. The neocloud sector collectively booked $23B to $25B in 2025 revenue, more than triple its 2024 total, as providers like CoreWeave expand into sovereign datacenters and managed services to challenge hyperscalers on enterprise AI inference.

title: iPhone 17 won’t get iOS 27’s top-tier features, here’s why
blurb: The base iPhone 17 won't get iOS 27's most powerful on-device AI model or Siri voice customization, both gated to 12GB RAM.

title: Deep Space Network antenna mishap blamed on poor training and procedures
blurb: A NASA investigation blamed $4.1M to $4.6M in damage to its 70-meter DSS-14 Deep Space Network antenna at Goldstone on poor training and inadequate procedures, after the dish over-rotated and flooded its base with more than 750,000 liters of water and glycol. The report faulted reliance on "personal heroics" and undocumented institutional knowledge, and the antenna has been offline since the September 16 incident.

title: This CarPlay change may be the missing link for Tesla support
blurb: Apple highlighted a CarPlay route-sharing feature in iOS 26.4 that lets navigation apps pass routing data to the vehicle, a capability seen as the likely missing piece for Tesla's rumored CarPlay support. The technology, surfaced during a WWDC developer session, matters for EVs because it ties charging stops and battery level into navigation, and it is available to developers now rather than in a future release.

title: Apple explains why Siri’s major iOS 27 overhaul took so long
blurb: Apple's Siri lead Mike Rockwell said the iOS 27 Siri overhaul slipped because the company scrapped an early version that bolted tool calling onto the old Siri and rebuilt the assistant "from the ground up" on its new models. The result is a natively multimodal Siri with its own app that runs the same across iPhone, iPad, Mac, Watch, Vision Pro, CarPlay, and AirPods.

title: Intel's one-two punch plan in desktop CPUs is taking shape — Z990 spotted, Nova Lake detailed, ‘Raptor Lake Next’ teased
blurb: At Computex 2026 Intel detailed its desktop roadmap: Nova Lake at CES 2027, an LGA 1700 "Raptor Lake Next" refresh, and a 52-core flagship.

title: Finland charges Russian captain and crew member of ship suspected of damaging undersea cables — prosecutors claim ship had eight more targets before it was stopped by coast guard
blurb: Finland charged the Russian captain and Azerbaijani bosun of the cargo ship Fitburg with aggravated criminal mischief, alleging the vessel dragged its anchor over 80 miles and severed two undersea cables linking Finland and Estonia. Prosecutors say the ship intended to hit eight more cables before the coast guard stopped it, marking Finland's second sabotage prosecution after a 2024 case collapsed on jurisdictional grounds.

title: Mechanical Watch (2022)
blurb: Bartosz Ciechanowski's interactive explainer breaks down how a purely mechanical watch keeps time, walking through the seven-part timekeeping system that converts stored spring energy into the steady rotation of the second hand. Draggable 3D models and animations let readers peek inside the movement, tracing power from the twisted spiral torsion spring through each gear and escapement without a single battery or electronic component.

title: Commodore Releases Flip Phone
blurb: Commodore launched the Callback, a flip phone that runs messaging and music apps but blocks social media and browsers at the system level.

title: Commodore gets into the phone biz with Sailfish-powered retro 'Callback'
blurb: Commodore is launching the Callback, a retro-styled smartphone running the Linux-based Sailfish OS instead of Android or iOS.

title: How Elon Musk's second-in-command Gwynne Shotwell helped turn SpaceX into an IPO giant
blurb: Gwynne Shotwell, SpaceX's president and COO and an early 2002 hire, rang the Nasdaq bell on Friday for the company's IPO and now holds a stake worth roughly $2 billion. She oversees SpaceX's 22,000-person workforce and its day-to-day operations, including Starlink and the xAI integration, while Musk sets high-level strategy and technical direction.

title: Samsung Display just showed why XR’s future may come down to better tiny screens
blurb: Samsung Display introduced a 1.3-inch RGB OLEDoS panel rated at 40,000 nits, targeting the brightness and efficiency demands of XR headsets and AR smart glasses. The technology builds OLED directly on a wafer and drops the color filter used in white OLEDoS, which Samsung says simplifies manufacturing and could enable thinner devices, though the company shared no product timelines, pricing, or customer names.

title: JAXA Nails H3 Return to Flight
blurb: JAXA launched its H3 rocket on its eighth mission Friday from Tanegashima Space Center, marking the first successful flight of the new three-engine variant. The vehicle delivered all six of its payloads to space, returning the program to flight after earlier setbacks and validating the upgraded configuration Japan plans to rely on for future launches.

title: Katalyst Space raises $12 million for GEO servicing demo mission
blurb: Katalyst Space Technologies raised $12M, led by Geodesic Capital, to build its first Nexus spacecraft for servicing satellites in geostationary orbit, with launch planned for 2027 on an Ariane 6. The round comes as Katalyst's Link spacecraft prepares for a late-June launch to attach to NASA's decaying Swift Observatory and raise its orbit, preventing an early reentry.

title: An Orbital StormWall Could Mitigate The Next Carrington Event
blurb: A paper in Space Weather proposes StormWall, a cloud of neutral alkali-metal gas released into geosynchronous orbit to blunt the next Carrington-class geomagnetic storm. Once ionized by the storm, the gas could absorb up to 50% of the incoming energy before it couples to Earth's magnetosphere, shielding the power grid, GEO satellites, and the ISS using roughly 384,000 kg of gas, or about six Starship launches.

title: Here’s everything new for Apple Photos in iOS 27
blurb: iOS 27 brings AI editing tools (Extend, Reframe, upgraded Clean Up), full-resolution Shared Albums, and cross-platform sharing to Apple Photos.

title: Sustained maneuver has a propulsion problem
blurb: Spacecraft architecture has long been treated as a problem of placement, optimizing where to position satellites and how reliably they hold station. The author argues that emerging missions demand sustained maneuvering instead of fixed orbits, and that today's propulsion systems cannot supply the continuous thrust those operations require.

title: Apple faces yet another EU antitrust investigation, this time for preferencing iCloud
blurb: Italy's competition regulator opened a DMA investigation into whether Apple illegally preferences iCloud over rival cloud services.

title: Commodore announces Linux-based flip phone with ‘no social media, no browser’ — the Callback 8020 will be available in five retro colorways starting at $499, runs 99% of Android apps
blurb: Commodore announced the Callback 8020, a Linux-based flip phone that hard-blocks browsers, social media, and work apps to encourage a digital detox. Built on a custom version of Jolla's Sailfish OS, it still runs 99% of Android apps and starts at $499, with pre-orders opening June 30th across five retro colorways.

title: The all-new Google Home speaker has finally arrived for $100
blurb: Google opened pre-orders for an all-new $100 Google Home speaker, with 360-degree audio in a compact chassis and an official on-sale date of June 25. The bigger shift is that Gemini replaces Google Assistant as the default, handling multi-step commands and retaining more context across a conversation, though unlocking full functionality requires a $10-a-month Google Home Premium subscription.

title: How Many Shots Are Enough for a Quantum Circuit?
blurb: A black-box framework, IncrementalExecution, decides when to stop running quantum circuit shots once added executions stop changing the output distribution.

title: The Secret to Marathon-Winning Humanoid Robots
blurb: The Honor Lightning humanoid ran a half-marathon in 50:26, and this analysis traces its win to optimal gear-ratio choices rather than novel technology.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that exposes Claude as a server-side language model conforming to Apple's Foundation Models LanguageModel protocol, so the same LanguageModelSession API drives respond(to:), streaming, guided generation, and tool calling. Requests go directly from the app to the Claude API at standard Anthropic pricing with Apple outside the request path, and the package targets the OS 27 beta API while remaining in beta itself.

title: 144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
blurb: Attackers hijacked a former Mastra contributor's npm account to publish 144 malicious packages carrying a crypto-stealing trojan via a postinstall hook.

title: Qwen-Robot Suite: A Foundation Model Suite for Physical World Intelligence
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-Robot Suite, a family of foundation models aimed at physical world intelligence and robotic control. The suite extends Qwen's vision-language work into embodied settings, targeting perception, planning, and action so robots can interpret their surroundings and execute multi-step tasks in the real world.

title: Z.ai’s open-weights GLM-5.2 beats GPT-5.5 on multiple long-horizon coding benchmarks for 1/6th the cost
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, a 753B-parameter open-weights model under an MIT license that beats GPT-5.5 on several long-horizon coding benchmarks at roughly one-sixth the cost. It ships with a 1M-token context window, $12.60-per-month enterprise tiers, and an IndexShare architecture that reuses one indexer across every four sparse attention layers to cut per-token compute by 2.9x.

title: Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment
blurb: OpenAI introduced Deployment Simulation, a method that replays prior conversations in a privacy-preserving way against a candidate model to estimate how it will behave before release. Across multiple GPT-5-series Thinking deployments, it improved estimates of undesired behavior rates, surfaced novel forms of misalignment, and reduced the risk that models could tell they were being tested.

title: Amazon S3 annotations: attach rich, queryable context directly to your objects
blurb: Amazon S3 introduced annotations, a metadata capability that lets you attach up to 1,000 named annotations per object, each up to 1 MB and totaling 1 GB, in JSON, XML, YAML, or plain text. Annotations can be modified or deleted without rewriting objects, travel with objects during copy and replication, and flow into managed tables that are queryable with Amazon Athena when S3 Metadata is enabled.

title: GLM-5.2 is the new leading open weights model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, which scores 51 on Artificial Analysis's Intelligence Index v4.1 to become the leading open weights model, ahead of MiniMax-M3 and DeepSeek V4 Pro (both 44). The MIT-licensed model keeps GLM-5.1's 744B total / 40B active parameters but extends the context window to 1M tokens, with first-party pricing at $1.4/$4.4 per 1M input/output tokens.

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
blurb: Google released the final version of Android 17 alongside Wear OS 7 and a Pixel Drop that adds new AI models, including the Lyria 3 music generator, multimodal Gemini Omni for conversational video editing, and AudioLM speech-to-speech translation on the Pixel 10a. The OS also introduces a "bubble bar" for faster multi-app workflows and emergency detection on Pixel Watch for crashes, falls, and missing pulse.

title: OpenAI prepares major ChatGPT voice upgrade with GPT-Bidi-1
blurb: OpenAI is preparing a major ChatGPT voice upgrade built around a next-generation audio model tentatively named GPT-Bidi-1, designed to listen and speak simultaneously, absorb interruptions, and adjust mid-sentence. The bidirectional model would add High, Medium, and Instant intelligence tiers mirroring the text side, closing a gap that left voice on an older stack while text reached the GPT-5.5 generation.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: NVIDIA's engineering blog details advanced fusion kernels that increase training throughput for mixture-of-experts (MoE) models by combining multiple operations into single GPU kernels to cut memory traffic and launch overhead. The post covers fused kernels for routing, grouped GEMMs, and activation functions, showing measurable speedups for the expert layers that dominate MoE compute.

title: Context engineering is the new analytics engineering skill: a practical guide for dbt users
blurb: Analytics engineers already practice context engineering through dbt metric definitions, model descriptions, and schema contracts that make business logic machine-readable.

title: The State of Fable, The Jailbreak Problem, SpaceX Acquires Cursor
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that while the administration is very likely wrong in its assessment of Fable, the burden of proof still falls on Anthropic to demonstrate otherwise. The piece ties that question to the broader jailbreak problem, contending that responsibility for model misuse and the perception of it ultimately rests with the labs building the systems, not the regulators reacting to them.

title: Macron Seeks Way Around Trump’s Ban on Anthropic’s AI Models
blurb: French President Emmanuel Macron will lead talks with G7 leaders and tech executives on deploying advanced AI models through "trusted partners," after the US blocked foreign nationals from accessing Anthropic's technology. The trusted-partner approach is being explored as a workaround that would let allied nations use the most capable models without directly violating the American export restrictions.

title: From the Hugging Face Hub to robot hardware with Strands Agents and LeRobot
blurb: Strands Robots is an open source SDK from AWS that exposes robot abstractions, simulation, and the LeRobot stack as composable AgentTools, letting you record demonstrations, train, run policy inference, and coordinate a fleet from a single Strands agent. The integration stays thin, keeping LeRobot's dataset format intact while the same agent code moves from simulation to a physical SO-101 with one keyword argument change.

title: Unlocking UK house-building with AI-accelerated planning
blurb: Google DeepMind partnered with the UK government to build a Gemini-powered prototype that helps planning officers process householder applications, aiming to cut decision times by 50%. Built alongside Google Cloud, Faculty, and councils in Barnet, Camden, and Dorset, the tool automates data extraction and case analysis, and the government plans to make it available to all councils nationally from 2027.

title: Import AI 461: "Alignment is not on track"; FrontierCode; and synthetic research interns
blurb: The latest issue of Jack Clark's Import AI leads with the argument that AI alignment is not on track, contending that capability gains are outpacing the safety techniques meant to keep advanced systems controllable. It also covers FrontierCode, a benchmark for evaluating models on difficult coding tasks, and early experiments with synthetic research interns, AI agents tasked with running portions of the research process autonomously.

title: SubQ 1.1 Small
blurb: SubQ released the model card for SubQ 1.1 Small, the second iteration of its Subquadratic Sparse Attention (SSA) model, which hits near-perfect needle-in-a-haystack retrieval up to 12M tokens. At 1M tokens, it requires 64.5x less compute than dense attention and runs 56x faster than FlashAttention-2, with broader 2M-to-12M-token models planned later this year.

title: Z.ai pitches GLM-5.2 for long-running software engineering tasks
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-source model built for long-running software engineering tasks across repository-scale codebases. The model pairs a one million-token context window with IndexShare, a technique that cuts per-token compute by 2.9x at full context, and Z.ai says it trails Claude Opus 4.8 by 1% on the FrontierSWE long-horizon coding benchmark.

title: Anthropic's Fable shutdown is a big moment for open-source AI
blurb: Anthropic pulled access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all customers late Friday to comply with a US export control directive citing national security authorities. The abrupt cutoff drove interest in downloadable open-source models that companies can run on their own infrastructure, with Chinese labs MiniMax and Zhipu surging on Monday as developers weighed the risk of single-vendor lock-in.

title: June Pixel Drop: New features for creators, Gemini upgrades and more
