blurb: The latest issue of CSS-Tricks' What's !important roundup covers the new `@function` rule for authoring custom CSS functions, the `alpha()` function for adjusting opacity on existing colors, and Grid Lanes for more flexible track placement. It also surfaces lesser-known `dialog` element behaviors and a CSS-only Wordle build worth studying for layout tricks.

title: ‘Hard Fork’ Live Part 2: Dylan Field on Standing Out in the A.I. Era
blurb: Figma CEO Dylan Field argued on a live "Hard Fork" episode that designers and writers with a distinct creative voice should take public risks now, framing the AI era as an opening rather than a threat. His pitch reframes the standout-or-fade anxiety around AI tooling as a case for putting personal work out there, betting that human voice becomes more valuable as generation gets cheaper.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD appears to have silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, stripping protection against physical attacks that siphon data from connected memory, with the change traced to AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware. The removal is undetectable on Windows and hard to spot on Linux, so defenders relying on at-rest memory encryption for consumer endpoints should verify TSME status directly rather than assume it is still active.

title: Three critical Fortinet sandbox bugs splattered by unknown attackers
blurb: Three critical vulnerabilities in Fortinet's FortiSandbox are under active exploitation by unidentified attackers, with the flaws allowing remote code execution on the threat-analysis appliances. Organizations running FortiSandbox on reachable network segments should apply Fortinet's patches immediately and hunt for signs of compromise, since a foothold on the sandbox itself can blind detection and expose submitted malware samples and analysis data.

title: Steam Workshop abused to spread malware via Wallpaper Engine app
blurb: Threat actors are abusing Steam Workshop's Wallpaper Engine app to distribute malware through "application" wallpapers, which run executable Windows code on install, per Kaspersky. Active since at least late 2025, the malicious packages deploy a DarkKomet backdoor, a trojanized AggregatorHost.dll that steals Steam credentials, and Lumma and Vidar stealers, with some samples downloaded tens of thousands of times.

title: 144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
blurb: Attackers hijacked the dormant npm account of a former Mastra contributor ("ehindero") and mass-published 144 malicious "@mastra/*" packages on June 17, each adding a tainted "easy-day-js" dependency whose postinstall hook drops a cross-platform infostealer targeting browser history and 160+ crypto wallet extensions. Audit Mastra installs from the past 24 hours, rotate any exposed credentials and wallet keys, and pin to releases carrying SLSA provenance attestations, since the attacker published from a personal token without them.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48907 (CVSS 10.0), a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor (JCE), to its KEV catalog after confirming active exploitation that lets unauthenticated users create editor profiles and upload and execute arbitrary PHP code. The flaw affects JCE versions 1.0.0 through 2.9.99.4 and is patched in 2.9.99.5, with FCEB agencies ordered to remediate by June 19.

title: CISA orders feds to patch max severity Joomla plugin flaw by Friday
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin, to its KEV catalog and ordered FCEB agencies to patch by Friday under BOD 26-04. Unauthenticated attackers are exploiting it via automated attacks to upload and run PHP code, and JCE warns that patching to 2.9.99.6 closes the entry point but does not remove implants left on already-compromised sites.

title: Cisco patches SD-WAN flaw amid evidence of active exploitation
blurb: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262, a flaw in the web interface of Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (formerly vManage) that lets an authenticated attacker with write access upload crafted files to overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root, after observing limited exploitation in the wild. There are no workarounds, so admins should upgrade to fixed releases and review SD-WAN Manager logs for uploads of index.jsp or .war files.

title: 3 Recently Patched Fortinet FortiSandbox Vulnerabilities in Hacker Crosshairs
blurb: Three recently patched Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws are under active exploitation, with Defused and KEVIntel honeypots observing attacks on the critical CVE-2026-39813 (authentication bypass) and CVE-2026-39808 (OS command injection, RCE), plus the June-patched CVE-2026-25089 (unauthenticated command execution). Separately, SOCRadar's FortiBleed campaign has compromised over 30,000 Fortinet firewalls across 190 countries via password-spraying, so apply all available patches and rotate credentials on exposed appliances immediately.

title: Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats
blurb: Fifteen malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins, posing as DeepSeek-based AI coding assistants, have exfiltrated users' OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek API keys in plaintext to an attacker server (39.107.60.51) since late October 2025. Aikido Security found the plugins work as advertised while siphoning entered keys, with two—CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist—each showing over 25,000 possibly inflated downloads, so audit installs and rotate any keys exposed to them.

title: New Rokarolla Android malware targets 217 banking, crypto apps
blurb: New Rokarolla Android banking trojan targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps using 137 C2 commands, spreading via fake Chrome and TikTok installers that impersonate Google Play Protect to drop the payload. Once granted Accessibility permissions, it serves phishing overlays to steal credentials and card data, logs keystrokes, harvests SMS and contacts, and disables Play Protect, per Zimperium.

title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
blurb: Defused Cyber reports active exploitation of three Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089) over the past 24 hours, including a critical path traversal flaw in the JRPC API (CVE-2026-39813, CVSS 9.1) that Fortinet patched only last week. Operators running FortiSandbox appliances should apply the latest fixes immediately and hunt for path-traversal indicators against the JRPC API, treating exposed instances as potentially already compromised.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been fully ported to Android 17, with developer builds available now and the first official release scheduled for tomorrow across all supported Pixel devices, including the 6a, 7, 8, and 10 Pro Fold. The hardened mobile OS has been tested on each device ahead of public release, continuing its same-day tracking of upstream Android.

title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
blurb: Rokarolla, a new Android banking Trojan tracked by Zimperium zLabs, spreads via fake Chrome and TikTok download pages and steals credentials from 217 banking and crypto apps. Beyond theft, it wields 137 C2 commands for full device control, harvesting lock-screen credentials, contacts, SMS, and keystrokes while blocking calls, deploying overlays, and disabling Google Play Protect.

title: Microsoft working on Defender patch for RoguePlanet zero-day
blurb: Microsoft confirmed it's working on a fix for RoguePlanet (CVE-2026-50656), an unpatched elevation-of-privilege flaw in the Microsoft Defender Malware Protection Engine that abuses a race condition to spawn SYSTEM-level command prompts on fully patched Windows 10 and 11. A public proof-of-concept already exists and works regardless of real-time protection, so treat affected endpoints as exploitable until the engine update ships.

title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed "SearchLeak," a now-patched three-stage attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot that used parameter-to-prompt injection (P2P) to silently exfiltrate emails, OneDrive files, SharePoint documents, and MFA codes via a single malicious Copilot link. The attack bypassed guardrails and browser CSP by hiding the exfiltration URL in an image tag behind a Bing search-by-image link, triggering a server-side fetch from Bing's infrastructure before Microsoft could sanitize the prompt.

title: Windows and Linux users: The deadline to update Secure Boot keys is near
blurb: Three Microsoft-signed Secure Boot certificates that anchor the UEFI chain of trust expire starting June 24, after which affected Windows and Linux systems will no longer receive validly signed boot components or firmware updates. Administrators should deploy the updated key-exchange and database certificates before the deadline, since unpatched machines lose the signature checks that block bootkits capable of surviving OS reinstalls.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, with SOCRadar finding a verified repository of working credentials after discovering the attackers' exposed operational server. Researchers attribute this to credential compromise rather than a Fortinet flaw, with telecom, government, and critical infrastructure hardest hit, so defenders should rotate credentials on default, built-in, and long-lived admin accounts and enforce MFA on edge appliances.

title: China-Linked SprySOCKS Backdoor Expands to Windows with Driver-Based Stealth
blurb: ESET identified two previously undocumented Windows variants of SprySOCKS, a backdoor tied to China-linked activity and once thought to be Linux-only, internally marked WIN_DRV and WIN_PLUS. Both ship with hard-coded C&C configurations and communicate over TCP and UDP, with the WIN_DRV variant using a kernel driver to hide its presence on infected hosts.

title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-54420 (CVSS 8.5), a privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation for root access on affected hosts. FCEB agencies must remediate by June 18, 2026; operators running the plugin should patch immediately and audit for signs of root-level compromise on shared hosting environments.

title: Rokarolla Android trojan targets banking and crypto users, enables device takeover
blurb: Rokarolla, a newly discovered Android banking trojan tracked by Zimperium, targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and supports 137 C2 commands, spreading via fake TikTok and Chrome sites and a dropper posing as Google Play Protect. After abusing Accessibility Services, it serves phishing overlays to harvest credentials and card data, runs keyloggers, intercepts SMS, and disables Google Play Protect for full device takeover.

title: Apple is about to make Hide My Email useless
blurb: Apple will move Sign in with Apple and Hide My Email aliases to the @private.icloud.com subdomain, making relay addresses trivially distinguishable from regular iCloud mailboxes. The change lets services block all aliases without affecting real iCloud accounts, undermining the plausible deniability that made Hide My Email useful for privacy; existing @icloud.com aliases can still be generated until the switch lands.

title: Cloudflare DMARC Management is now generally available
blurb: Cloudflare made DMARC Management generally available for free to all customers, with a redesigned dashboard that guides domains to full DMARC enforcement without parsing raw XML aggregate reports. The tool unifies SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and BIMI posture in a single view, helping defenders move policies from p=none to quarantine or reject to block spoofing and brand impersonation.

title: Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw
blurb: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS 7.8), an authentication-bypass flaw in the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS, by an unknown threat actor gaining unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals. Organizations running affected PAN-OS versions on internet-facing GlobalProtect should patch immediately, audit portal access logs for unauthorized sessions, and rotate credentials on appliances that may already be compromised.

title: Microsoft confirms Office apps launch issues after June updates
blurb: Microsoft confirmed that Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026 break OLE automation, preventing third-party apps like Zotero, Dentrix, and CCH Engagement from launching Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access or opening documents, often with no error shown. No fix is available yet, so the interim workaround is to open Office files directly, with enterprise customers able to request a tenant-wide mitigation through Microsoft Support for Business.

title: FBI dismantles Chinese phishing service that coached buyers to generate scam sites using AI —$88 cybercrime product linked to $1.9 billion in losses, 3.87 million stolen cards
blurb: The FBI, Google, and Lumen Technologies dismantled Outsider Enterprise, a China-based phishing-as-a-service operation sold via a Telegram bot for $88 per week, seizing its servers and payment wallets under Operation Ghost Hook. Google's complaint alleges operators distributed tutorials coaching subscribers to bypass Gemini's safety filters by framing phishing-page code as an innocuous "gift redemption page," and ties the platform to 3.87 million stolen cards and $1.9 billion in losses since July 2023.

title: 1Password Acquires Apono in Reported $250M-$300M Deal
blurb: 1Password acquired Apono, an Israel-based just-in-time access governance startup, in a deal Calcalist reported at $250M to $300M. The acquisition extends 1Password's identity platform to broker temporary, narrowly scoped privileges for humans, machines, and AI agents, the latter tied to a human delegator and declared task intent with real-time drift monitoring.

title: 'Lorem Ipsum' Malware Pivots to ClickFix Delivery
blurb: The operators of the Lorem Ipsum shellcode loader have abandoned Trojanized Microsoft Teams installers for ClickFix lures hosted on compromised WordPress sites, after Microsoft's takedown of signing-as-a-service provider Fox Tempest revoked over 1,000 fraudulent Trusted Signing certificates. BlueVoyant now links the campaign to Rapid Brigantine (aka Vanilla Tempest, Vice Society), broadening the victim pool from installer downloaders to anyone browsing the infected sites.

title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
blurb: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262 (CVSS 6.5) in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, an authenticated arbitrary file write in the web UI that attackers are exploiting in the wild to overwrite files and escalate to root. Exploitation requires valid credentials with write access; defenders should patch to the fixed releases and audit /var/log/nms/vmanage-server.log for suspicious WAR file uploads.

title: AI Use by the US Government
blurb: The OMB disclosed 3,611 active or planned federal AI use cases as of April 14, a 70% increase over the final Biden-era inventory, spanning grant-application ideology screening at HHS, inmate-misconduct prediction at the Bureau of Prisons, and reactor control at the Department of Energy. The author argues most of these programs could be implemented responsibly, and that the real objections trace to the underlying policies rather than the use of automation itself.

title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
blurb: A China-linked espionage group lived inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks for over a year, deploying a backdoor on REDCap servers to harvest login credentials and exfiltrate sensitive research and defense email. Rather than using malware to pull data out, the attackers rewrote victims' own Google Workspace mail-forwarding rules to silently copy every message to attacker-controlled inboxes, evading endpoint detection.

title: Windows update leaves third-party Office document launches in limbo
blurb: A recent Windows update broke the file-association handlers that let third-party applications launch Microsoft Office documents, leaving those apps unable to open files they previously handled. Microsoft has acknowledged the regression but not yet shipped a fix, so admins relying on non-Microsoft document workflows should hold the update or prepare a rollback until a patch lands.

title: The Ghosts of Polymarket: When Off-Chain Matches Meet On-Chain Reverts
blurb: Researchers disclosed Ghost Fills, a class of attacks against Polymarket's hybrid architecture that matches orders off-chain but settles on-chain, where attackers exploit the timing gap to invalidate already-matched orders before finalization via four vectors (nonce bump, balance drain, allowance revoke, proxy trap). Across 1.95M reverted transactions they found 980,133 selectively reverted fills netting $1.49M and placing $1.78B at risk, with vulnerable contract code still present in 167 contracts across 10 chains holding $23M.

title: Rockwell Automation Patches Vulnerabilities in ICS Controllers and Software
blurb: Rockwell Automation released patches for multiple high- and critical-severity flaws across its Logix and CompactLogix controllers, Flex I/O Ethernet/IP adapters, RSLinx, and FactoryTalk suite, including authentication bypass, improper API authorization, and several non-recoverable DoS faults. None of the newly disclosed bugs are under active exploitation, but the critical Flex I/O flaw lets an unauthenticated attacker change a device's web password, so OT teams running edge-exposed adapters should prioritize patching.

title: Security Community Slams US Ban on Exporting Mythos, Fable
blurb: Dozens of security experts signed an open letter urging the US government to reverse export controls that bar foreign nationals from using Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, restrictions that forced Anthropic to suspend both models on June 12 for compliance. The government has not stated a specific reason, though Semafor reported the order followed White House suspicion that a China-linked group accessed Mythos, which Anthropic says can discover critical vulnerabilities and build novel exploits.

title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
blurb: ScarCruft (APT37), a North Korean state-sponsored group, is running a spear-phishing campaign that impersonates Microsoft Account security alerts to trick recipients into deploying NarwhalRAT. The lures play on account-compromise fear to drive execution, so defenders should flag inbound mail spoofing Microsoft security notifications and hunt for NarwhalRAT delivery artifacts on endpoints.

title: Stop Using JWTs
blurb: The author argues developers should stop using JWTs for user session management and use regular cookie sessions instead. The case rests on a few points: the JWT spec targets very short-lived tokens (~5 minutes), secure "stateless" auth is not actually feasible since you need server-side state to revoke tokens, and a JWT carrying only a session ID gains nothing over a plain session cookie while adding spec-level risk. Google's JWT usage is SSO transport between hosts, not browser sessions.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: Curl maintainer Daniel Stenberg announced the project will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026, pausing its bug bounty program for the month to give maintainers a break from the volume of low-quality, often AI-generated submissions. Reports submitted during the window will be rejected and must be resubmitted in August, when normal triage and bounty payouts resume.

title: Apple collects every tap to deliver App Store personalized recommendations
blurb: Security researchers at Mysk found that Apple's App Store logs every tap users make to power its new Personalized Collections feature, capturing identifiable analytics that can even derive a user's typing speed during searches, with no setting to disable the collection. The feature began rolling out this week in the U.S., and Apple currently offers no opt-out toggle for the underlying data capture.

title: Cardiac monitor maker's security skips a beat as data thieves go for the jugular
blurb: iRhythm Technologies, a California cardiac-monitoring firm, disclosed that attackers used social engineering against third-party business applications to exfiltrate protected health information and proprietary data, with an extortion demand received June 9 and the breach deemed material by June 10. The company says clinical systems and medical devices were unaffected and has not disclosed how many individuals are impacted, while no major ransomware group has claimed the intrusion.

title: The FCC Wants to Eliminate Burner Phones
blurb: The FCC proposed a rule requiring US telecoms to collect and store personal data on nearly all phone customers, including a government-issued ID number and physical address, effectively eliminating burner phones whose accounts aren't tied to an identity. The agency frames the mandate as an anti-scam measure, but privacy advocates warn it builds a centralized trove of identity data with significant surveillance and cybersecurity knock-on risks.

title: What’s new in Databricks Platform security and compliance at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks announced new platform security capabilities at Data + AI Summit 2026, including general availability of Automatic Identity Management for Microsoft Entra ID on AWS and GCP, AIM for Okta in public preview, and Context-Based Ingress policies. The release also adds Private Network Gateway and expanded Private Link support for Lakebase, plus broader AWS GovCloud coverage for AI services and upcoming FedRAMP High support on Azure Commercial.

title: Apple plans to change its Hide My Email privacy feature that could make it less effective
blurb: Apple will move Hide My Email's anonymized addresses from @icloud.com to a distinct @private.icloud.com domain in the coming weeks, letting apps and websites identify and block the masked sign-ups the feature was designed to obscure. Existing addresses keep forwarding, but developers must update mail filtering to avoid dropping messages, and the change lands months after Apple disclosed a Hide My Email user's real identity under subpoena.

title: India's Telegram ban hit the UAE too. Here's how to get around it
blurb: India banned Telegram nationwide until June 22 under Section 69A of the IT Act, but the block spilled across borders after Reliance's AS18101 began announcing Telegram's BGP prefixes, dropping access for users as far as the UAE. Kentik's Doug Madory confirmed the route hijack and noted RPKI origin validation limited its spread, though FLAG Telecom (AS15412) failed to filter the invalid announcement; operators should reject unauthorized prefixes from AS18101.

title: Twenty, which uses AI to help US military hackers penetrate adversary computer networks, raised a $100M Series B led by Accel at a $1B valuation (Colin Demarest/Axios)
blurb: Twenty raised a $100M Series B led by Accel at a $1B valuation to scale its AI platform that helps US military hackers penetrate adversary computer networks. The round signals continued investor appetite for offensive-cyber tooling built directly for government use, joining a growing cohort of defense-tech startups selling AI capabilities to the Pentagon and intelligence agencies.

title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from user-to-user social platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and X starting in early 2027, with possible limits on infinite scrolling and digital curfews for under-18s. Privacy experts warn the mandate could push platforms toward intrusive age-verification that normalizes identity collection and surveillance, mirroring bans already in force in Australia and proposed in Canada and several EU states.

title: AI’s constant patching treadmill can be a security problem
blurb: Backslash Security reviewed two months of Claude Code update logs and found Anthropic silently shipped 30-plus security-relevant patches between April and early June 2026, covering prompt injection, data poisoning, OAuth credential leakage, and arbitrary code execution. One fix closed a bypass where adding a single backslash defeated guardrails against catastrophic deletion commands. The takeaway: AI coding tools patch at a far higher cadence than traditional software, so enable auto-updates and track changelogs for undisclosed fixes.

title: Jamf announces new native AI Governance control plane for macOS
blurb: Jamf announced AI Governance, an upcoming Jamf Pro capability that gives IT and security teams OS-level visibility and control over generative AI tools running on managed Macs, including local models and developer tooling that network proxies miss. It offers deep tool discovery, granular policy controls across model access, network, and file system, and Model Context Protocol server restrictions, plus a vendor engine that tracks new controls automatically.

title: Di5Guise: 5G Privacy with vSIM
blurb: Researchers introduced Di5Guise, a privacy-enhancing 5G architecture that breaks the correlation between device identity and subscriber identity exploited by an operator-level adversary to deanonymize users across rotated profiles. Its core component, vSIM, is a virtualized SIM running on attested secure hardware that provisions a distinct, unlinkable device profile per subscriber, cutting re-identification accuracy from 93% to 49% when paired with traffic obfuscation on srsRAN-based 5G infrastructure.

title: SentinelOne turns Purple AI loose to investigate threats on its own
blurb: SentinelOne opened its Purple AI Agentic Investigations capability to all Singularity Platform customers, adding "zero-click" autonomous threat investigation that triages, reaches a verdict, and can move to contain a threat without an analyst launching it. The feature runs on existing endpoint, identity, cloud, and third-party telemetry, uses a mix of Claude, GPT, and SentinelOne's Ultraviolet models, and ships with an adjustable human-in-the-loop control plus an auditable evidence chain.

title: Oracle’s Second Monthly Security Updates Deliver 245 Patches
blurb: Oracle released its June 2026 monthly Critical Security Patch Update with 245 fixes, roughly 120 rated critical and about 100 remotely exploitable without authentication, with more than 100 patches landing in Fusion Middleware. The advisory includes CVE-2026-35273, a PeopleSoft flaw that ShinyHunters has reportedly exploited against at least 100 organizations in the education sector, though Oracle does not confirm active exploitation.

title: Cybersecurity experts don’t think Anthropic’s Fable 5 presents a unique threat
blurb: Dozens of cybersecurity practitioners said Anthropic's Fable 5 offers no unique hacking capabilities, arguing recent jailbreak reports show nothing beyond what existing tools and models already provide. They called the US export controls on foreign use of Fable misguided, contending the restrictions target a threat the model does not meaningfully advance.

title: Tenet Security Emerges From Stealth With $6 Million Seed Funding
blurb: Tenet Security emerged from stealth with $6M in seed funding led by the Westly Group to detect and stop dangerous AI agent behavior at runtime. Founded by Unit 8200 alumni Barak Sternberg and Nevo Poran, who previously built Cisco's AI Defense research team, the firm uses a lightweight runtime sensor to monitor OS behavior, network and API calls, and agent LLM reasoning, flagging runaway or hijacked agents before harmful actions execute.

title: Unicorn – The Ultimate CPU Emulator
blurb: Unicorn Engine, the lightweight CPU emulator framework widely used for malware analysis and reverse engineering, won Alibaba Cloud's Asian Star 10x10 Award.

title: A case for how to shape ‘ingredient lists’ for AI models
blurb: A policy paper from the Institute for Security and Technology argues that AI bills of materials (AIBOMs), inventories of the models, datasets, and tuning sources behind a system, need foundational standards before broad adoption to avoid fragmented, incompatible implementations. The authors, including former government SBOM lead Allan Friedman, target Hill aides and Pentagon staffers and call for parallel supply- and demand-side requirements to break the chicken-and-egg adoption problem.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
blurb: CISA added a maximum-severity Joomla Content Editor flaw (CVE-2026-48907, CVSS 10.0) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation that lets unauthenticated users upload and run arbitrary PHP code. The bug affects JCE versions 1.0.0 through 2.9.99.4, was patched in 2.9.99.5 on June 3, and federal civilian agencies must apply the fix by June 19.

title: Three critical Fortinet sandbox bugs splattered by unknown attackers
blurb: Fortinet disclosed three critical vulnerabilities in its FortiSandbox malware-analysis appliance, all of which unknown attackers are already exploiting in the wild. Enterprise security teams running FortiSandbox should patch immediately, since the flaws sit in a tool meant to detect threats, and active exploitation gives intruders a foothold in the part of the stack defenders rely on to catch them.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD quietly removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs after the AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware update, reserving the memory-encryption feature for its Pro lineup without notifying users. The removal is undetectable on Windows and leaves non-Pro chips exposed to physical memory attacks, pushing buyers who need hardware encryption toward AMD's pricier Pro processors or commercial-tier procurement.

title: 3 Recently Patched Fortinet FortiSandbox Vulnerabilities in Hacker Crosshairs
blurb: Exploit intelligence firm Defused reported in-the-wild attacks targeting three recently patched Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws, including CVE-2026-39813, a critical authentication bypass, and CVE-2026-39808, a critical OS command injection bug, both patched in April. Separately, SOCRadar identified more than 30,000 Fortinet firewalls compromised in a self-propagating credential-harvesting campaign dubbed FortiBleed spanning organizations across 190-plus countries.

title: CISA orders feds to patch max severity Joomla plugin flaw by Friday
blurb: CISA added a maximum-severity flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin (CVE-2026-48907) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and gave federal civilian agencies until Friday to patch under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The unauthenticated bug allows PHP code execution, is under active automated exploitation with public exploit code, and patching alone does not remove implants from already-compromised installations.

title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48172, an actively exploited privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and gave federal civilian agencies three days to patch under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The symlink-following bug lets attackers with FTP or web shell access escalate to root on shared CloudLinux/CageFS hosting, affecting all plugin versions before 2.4.8.

title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google and Mozilla shipped browser updates patching more than 70 vulnerabilities across Chrome and Firefox, including critical use-after-free memory bugs that could enable remote code execution and sandbox escape. Chrome 149 fixes 33 defects and Firefox 152 fixes 40, with Mozilla also updating Firefox ESR, Thunderbird, and Firefox for iOS, making this a broad patch cycle for IT teams managing fleet browser deployments.

title: Crooks found a new way to collaborate using Teams – by hiding command-and-control traffic
blurb: Attackers are using Microsoft Teams as a covert channel for command-and-control traffic, blending malicious instructions into the platform's API calls so they pass as legitimate collaboration activity. The technique exploits the trust enterprises place in sanctioned SaaS traffic, putting pressure on security teams to monitor Teams and other M365 services for behavioral anomalies rather than relying on perimeter or domain-based detection.

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
blurb: Google released the final version of Android 17 and Wear OS 7, rolling out first to Pixel devices alongside a Pixel Drop that adds Gemini Omni video editing, Lyria 3 music generation, and AudioLM speech translation. The release doubles down on Google's strategy of using Android and Pixel as the showcase for its newest AI models, pressuring Apple ahead of its September Siri and iOS 27 upgrades.

title: Microsoft working on Defender patch for RoguePlanet zero-day
blurb: Microsoft assigned CVE-2026-50656 to the RoguePlanet zero-day and confirmed it is building a Defender patch, a week after researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept that spawns SYSTEM-level command prompts on fully patched Windows 10 and 11 via a Malware Protection Engine race condition. The flaw works whether or not real-time protection is enabled, leaving enterprise endpoints exposed in the gap until Microsoft ships a fix.

title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting three critical FortiSandbox flaws (CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089, all CVSS 9.1), enabling unauthenticated path traversal and command injection via crafted HTTP requests. Fortinet patched the first two in April 2026 and the third only last week, so IT teams running FortiSandbox, FortiSandbox Cloud, or PaaS deployments should apply the latest fixes immediately.

title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
blurb: Researchers disclosed "SearchLeak," a three-stage prompt-injection flaw in Microsoft Copilot that let attackers exfiltrate enterprise data through a single click using hidden URLs and crafted variables. Microsoft has patched the issue, but it joins a growing class of AI prompt-injection vulnerabilities that puts pressure on IT teams to vet Copilot deployments and agent-facing data flows before broad rollout.

title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
blurb: Zimperium zLabs researchers documented Rokarolla, an Android banking Trojan distributed through fake Chrome and TikTok downloads that targets 217 banking and crypto apps while using 137 commands to seize full administrative control of infected devices. Beyond credential theft, it harvests lock-screen passwords, logs keystrokes, blocks calls, deploys fraudulent overlays, and disables Google Play Protect, raising the stakes for enterprise mobile device management and BYOD policies.

title: China-Linked SprySOCKS Backdoor Expands to Windows with Driver-Based Stealth
blurb: ESET documented two Windows variants of SprySOCKS, a backdoor previously seen only on Linux and tied to the China-linked espionage group Earth Lusca, with one variant using a kernel driver to hide its processes, files, registry keys, and network connections. The malware supports over 30 commands and diverts TCP traffic through random ports to mask its listening port, raising the detection bar for enterprise endpoint and network monitoring tools.

title: Windows and Linux users: The deadline to update Secure Boot keys is near
blurb: Three Microsoft-signed Secure Boot certificates expire on June 24, after which firmware and OS components verified against them will no longer chain to a trusted root unless administrators provision the updated keys. IT teams that miss the window risk boot failures or Secure Boot fallback on Windows and Linux fleets, reopening the door to UEFI bootkits that load before the OS and survive reinstalls.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, harvesting verified working credentials rather than exploiting any Fortinet flaw, according to SOCRadar. Telecommunications, government, and critical-infrastructure organizations dominate the victim list, and over 20% of affected devices belong to enterprises with more than $1B in annual revenue, putting weak default and long-lived admin accounts squarely in scope for credential audits.

title: Microsoft confirms Office apps launch issues after June updates
blurb: Microsoft confirmed that Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026 break OLE automation, preventing third-party applications like CCH Engagement, Zotero, and dental software Dentrix from launching Office apps or opening Word, Excel, and PowerPoint documents. No fix is available yet, so Microsoft is directing affected enterprises to open documents directly or contact Support for Business for an org-wide workaround.

title: One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a now-patched flaw in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that chained three bugs into a one-click path for exfiltrating emails, calendar data, indexed files, and MFA codes. Because the malicious link resolved to a legitimate microsoft.com domain, conventional anti-phishing and URL-filtering tools failed to flag it, leaving enterprises reliant on Microsoft's server-side fix rather than their own controls.

title: AMD's Mext buy shows how AI could solve the RAM shortage it created
blurb: AMD acquired Mext, a memory-optimization startup, betting that smarter software-driven RAM tiering can ease the DRAM scarcity that AI workloads have driven to record prices. For enterprise IT buyers facing inflated server-memory costs and constrained capacity, the deal signals vendors are moving to squeeze more usable memory out of existing silicon rather than waiting on new fab supply to catch up.

title: Rokarolla Android trojan targets banking and crypto users, enables device takeover
blurb: Zimperium researchers disclosed Rokarolla, an Android banking trojan that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and can execute 137 commands on infected devices, including credential harvesting, SMS interception, and full device takeover. Distributed through fake TikTok and Chrome download sites and a dropper posing as Google Play Protect, it abuses Accessibility Services to display phishing overlays and disable Play Protect, raising the stakes for enterprise mobile-device management and BYOD security controls.

title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-54420, a privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, giving federal agencies until June 18 to patch. The 8.5-severity bug lets a user with FTP or web shell access escalate to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux or CageFS, and admins should upgrade to LiteSpeed WHM Plugin v5.3.2.1 or higher.

title: Cloudflare DMARC Management is now generally available
blurb: Cloudflare made DMARC Management generally available, offering a redesigned dashboard that gives every customer a unified view of their email authentication posture and a guided path to full DMARC enforcement at no cost. The free tooling parses XML aggregate reports, surfaces SPF and DKIM gaps, and lets IT teams move toward reject policies without hiring an email security consultant.

title: Tesco is sprinting to quit VMware and Broadcom despite rapid migration risks
blurb: Tesco is accelerating its exit from VMware and Broadcom, pushing an aggressive migration timeline despite the operational risk of moving production workloads quickly off the virtualization stack. The retailer's sprint reflects broad enterprise backlash against Broadcom's post-acquisition VMware licensing changes, which bundled products and raised costs, pushing large customers toward alternatives like Nutanix, Proxmox, and KVM-based platforms.

title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from social media starting spring 2027, requiring platforms including Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat, Facebook, and X to verify ages via ID upload or facial scans, effectively ending anonymous account creation for new users. Security and privacy experts warn the checks are easily circumvented and concentrate ID and biometric data in breach-prone systems, raising compliance and liability questions for any platform operating in the UK market.

title: Intel’s performance-enhanced 18A-P process enters risk production — drop-in 18A upgrade promises 9% performance improvement at iso-power, cuts thermal resistance by 40%
blurb: Intel’s 18A-P, a drop-in upgrade to 18A promising 9% more performance at iso-power, entered risk production.

title: AMD acquires storage software startup MEXT
blurb: AMD acquired storage software startup MEXT, whose Predictive Memory software makes flash appear as DRAM to the operating system, expanding usable memory capacity while preserving flash economics. The deal pairs MEXT's technology with AMD EPYC processors to cut server memory costs as DRAM prices climb, targeting AI and data center workloads where memory has become a primary performance and cost constraint.

title: AI Use by the US Government
blurb: The Office of Management and Budget disclosed 3,611 active or planned federal AI use cases, a 70% jump from the final Biden-era inventory, spanning grant screening at HHS via Palantir, inmate risk scoring at the Bureau of Prisons, and reactor control testing at the Department of Energy. The scale signals expanding federal demand for AI vendors and integrators, alongside procurement and oversight questions as agencies push automated decision-making into high-stakes domains.

title: Brit competition cops order Google to make search rankings less mysterious
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority ordered Google to disclose how it ranks search results and to give businesses clearer notice before ranking changes, using new powers under its Strategic Market Status designation for the search giant. The transparency mandate gives enterprises and publishers a basis to contest ranking shifts that affect traffic, and signals the CMA will police Google's search dominance more aggressively than past voluntary commitments allowed.

title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
blurb: A China-linked espionage group spent more than a year inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks, planting a credential-stealing backdoor on victims' REDCap servers and then rewiring their own Google Workspace mail rules to auto-forward sensitive research and defense email. The exfiltration technique abused trusted, native Workspace functionality, meaning IT teams that monitor for malware or external tooling would see legitimate mail-routing rules rather than an obvious breach.

title: 1Password Acquires Apono in Reported $250M-$300M Deal
blurb: 1Password acquired Apono, an Israeli just-in-time access governance vendor, in a deal Calcalist reports at $250M to $300M. The buy adds privileged access management for humans, machines, and AI agents to 1Password's identity platform, extending it from credential storage into the access-broker layer alongside its existing SaaS access and device-trust tooling.

title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
blurb: Cisco released patches for CVE-2026-20262, a medium-severity flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (formerly vManage) that attackers have already exploited in the wild to overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root. Exploitation requires valid credentials with write access, but IT teams running on-prem, cloud, or FedRAMP deployments should patch immediately and audit vManage server logs for suspicious WAR file uploads.

title: Windows update leaves third-party Office document launches in limbo
blurb: A recent Windows update broke the ability of third-party applications to launch Office document types, leaving non-Microsoft document handlers in an inconsistent state. IT teams running mixed productivity stacks face help-desk tickets and broken file associations until Microsoft ships a fix, reinforcing the operational cost of managing apps outside the native Microsoft 365 ecosystem.

title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
blurb: North Korea's ScarCruft group (APT37) is running spear-phishing campaigns that impersonate Microsoft Account security alerts, warning of "abnormal activity" and OTP abuse to trick recipients into opening a malicious ZIP attachment that deploys NarwhalRAT. The Python-based malware logs keystrokes, captures screenshots, records audio, and runs in memory via scheduled tasks, marking APT37's departure from its long-attributed RokRAT family.

title: Control whether your users can have temporary chats and delete conversations in the Gemini app
blurb: Google added admin controls for temporary chats and conversation deletion in the Gemini app, both ON by default and configurable at the domain, OU, or group level for all Workspace customers. The temporary-chat option lets users hold conversations that bypass history, while deletion controls give admins a governance lever alongside Vault's new retention rules and litigation holds for Gemini.

title: HPE Discover: Neri outlines an AI architecture built for agents
blurb: At HPE Discover 2026, CEO Antonio Neri detailed an agent-oriented AI architecture spanning the full stack, including Private Cloud AI scaling to 256 GPUs, the Juniper-derived networking refresh, and the MCP-native Alletra MPX 10,000 storage layer. The pitch adds agentic governance controls like zero-code agent registration, a three-tier identity model, and Zerto rollback, positioning HPE's Juniper-integrated portfolio against Dell and Nutanix for enterprise agent infrastructure.

title: From RAG to ontology: Databricks bets on context as the key to trusted AI agents
blurb: Databricks introduced Genie Ontology, a preview context layer that extracts business definitions from enterprise data, dashboards, and pipelines into a graph that AI agents query, using a PageRank-style system to rank which source an agent should trust. The pitch to CIOs is consistency and lineage that RAG and vector search can't provide, but analysts say data quality and governance will determine whether the tool actually improves trust enough to drive agent adoption.

title: AMD acquires MEXT to add predictive memory optimization to its AI stack
blurb: AMD acquired memory-optimization startup MEXT, adding software that uses predictive algorithms to shuttle hot data between flash and DRAM and make flash behave more like memory. The deal targets a sharp enterprise pain point: Gartner forecasts a 130% rise in combined DRAM and SSD prices by end of 2026, pushing IT buyers toward software that extends effective memory capacity without proportional hardware spend.

title: Microsoft might put China’s DeepSeek inside Copilot to tame its AI bills
blurb: Microsoft is exploring a self-hosted, fine-tuned version of DeepSeek V4 to power Copilot Cowork in Microsoft 365, offering it as a cheaper, Azure-hosted alternative to the Anthropic and OpenAI models the agentic assistant runs on today. The move pairs with a shift to usage-based pricing for Cowork and signals a multi-model strategy that hedges Microsoft's dependence on any single lab, though using a Chinese model carries clear political risk as Washington weighs a DeepSeek ban.

title: AMD takes over MEXT for memory tiering tech that enables flash to appear as DRAM to applications — tech to 'address growing memory constraints' in the data center
blurb: AMD acquired MEXT, a startup whose AI-based memory tiering technology makes NAND flash appear as DRAM to applications, moving cold data to cheaper storage and prefetching it back before workloads request it. AMD plans to fold MEXT's Predictive Memory Engine into its data center portfolio, targeting memory-bound AI deployments where DRAM cost and capacity, not compute, increasingly cap how large workloads can run.

title: Intel starts cooking up enhanced 18A-P silicon for would-be foundry customers
blurb: Intel began producing test silicon on its enhanced 18A-P process, a higher-performance variant of 18A aimed at the external foundry customers it has struggled to win. Landing major chip designers on 18A-P is central to Intel's pitch that its foundry business can compete with TSMC, and early customer silicon is the proof point investors and prospective partners have been waiting for.

title: Chinese memory brands ditch Samsung and Micron for homegrown CXMT and YMTC silicon — Corsair, HP, and Dell are already adopting the China-produced DDR5 chips
blurb: Chinese memory brands including Gloway and KingBank are switching from Samsung, Micron, and SK hynix to domestic DDR5 silicon from CXMT and YMTC, and Corsair, HP, and Dell have already begun qualifying or shipping CXMT-based kits. Backed by government subsidies that prioritize local supply over margin, the shift gives enterprise buyers a memory source insulated from the Big Three's AI-driven pricing premiums.

title: Microsoft faces down sueball, capacity problems in series of challenges
blurb: Microsoft faces a shareholder class action alleging it made misleading statements about Copilot adoption, after converting just 15 million of its 450 million Microsoft 365 users to paid seats and seeing its stock drop roughly 10% on the January 28 earnings disclosure. Separately, GitHub is reportedly tapping AWS for extra capacity to handle agentic-development load that Azure migration hasn't absorbed, raising reliability questions for large deployments like NHS England's 500,000-plus Copilot licenses.

title: Cardiac monitor maker's security skips a beat as data thieves go for the jugular
blurb: iRhythm Technologies disclosed that attackers used social engineering to breach its business applications on June 8, stealing protected health information and proprietary company data before demanding payment to avoid public disclosure. The cardiac-monitoring vendor deemed the incident material and filed with the SEC on June 10, but has not named the threat actor or said how many patients were affected, and is relying on cyber insurance to absorb part of the cost.

title: DocLang aims to make documents readable by AI, not humans
blurb: IBM, Nvidia, and Red Hat launched the DocLang working group under the Linux Foundation's LF AI & Data project to build an open, vendor-neutral document format designed for LLM tokenizers rather than human readers. The spec, joined by ABBYY and Human Signal and built on the existing DocLing toolkit, targets the cost and reliability problems enterprises hit when feeding PDFs and scanned files into generative AI and agentic systems.

title: SMI's PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer SSDs coming next year, but severe NAND shortages will get even worse in 2027 as AI data centers swallow supply — An interview with Silicon Motion's SVP Nelson Duann
blurb: Silicon Motion will ship its first PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer drives next year, but warns that NAND shortages driving up SSD prices will worsen through 2027 as AI data centers absorb supply. The supply crunch already reshaped the market in a single quarter, lifting SMI's Q1 controller sales 105% year-over-year to $342.1M even as retail SSD demand fell on rising prices.

title: AMD’s massive SP7 socket for EPYC Venice and Intel’s gargantuan 9,324-pin socket for Diamond Rapids appear at Computex — SP7 and LGA9324-1 sockets will power the next generation of AI servers
blurb: AMD and Intel showed off their next-generation server sockets at Computex 2026, with AMD's SP7 supporting up to 256-core EPYC Venice chips, 16 DDR6 channels, 96 PCIe 6.0 lanes, and 1,400W peak power, while Intel's 9,324-pin LGA9324-1 will carry 192-core Xeon Diamond Rapids in 2027. The power and cooling demands point to mandatory liquid cooling and higher rack costs as enterprises plan AI-server refreshes around these platforms.

title: SentinelOne turns Purple AI loose to investigate threats on its own
blurb: SentinelOne opened its Purple AI Agentic Investigations capability to all Singularity Platform customers, adding zero-click autonomous threat investigation that detects, triages, and moves to contain threats without an analyst launching it. The feature runs on existing endpoint, identity, and cloud telemetry with adjustable human-in-the-loop controls, and arrives alongside Singularity Credits, a unified currency customers draw down for AI work across the platform.

title: Salesforce acquires Fin, formerly known as Intercom
blurb: Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire Fin, the customer-support agent vendor formerly known as Intercom, for roughly $3.6B, with the deal expected to close in Q4 of its fiscal 2027. The purchase extends Agentforce, which hit $1.2B in ARR last quarter, by adding Fin's Apex support model and fast-deploy packages aimed at SMB and commercial buyers who need quick time-to-value.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: The Linux kernel 7.1 release will drop support for Intel's 486 processors, raising the minimum x86 baseline to Pentium-class chips with TSC and CMPXCHG8B instructions. The change simplifies kernel maintenance but forces the handful of organizations still running 486-era embedded or industrial systems to freeze on older kernels or migrate hardware.

title: India's Telegram ban hit the UAE too. Here's how to get around it
blurb: India invoked Section 69A of the IT Act to block Telegram nationwide until June 22 after the platform was used to sell leaked exam materials, and the enforcement spilled across borders when Reliance's AS18101 hijacked Telegram's BGP routes and knocked out access as far as the UAE. Network analysts at Kentik confirmed the hijack, and Telegram has moved the Delhi High Court to challenge a ban that digital rights groups call constitutionally incompatible.

title: Meet the new Google Home Speaker, built for Gemini
blurb: Google released the Google Home Speaker, its first audio device built for Gemini, available for pre-order at $99.99 and shipping June 25. The Gemini for Home assistant handles multi-step commands, mid-sentence corrections, and conversational follow-ups, extending Google's Gemini footprint into the home-audio hardware that competes with Amazon Echo and Apple HomePod.

title: Google releases Android 17 for Pixel phones
blurb: Google released the stable build of Android 17, rolling out to Pixel 6 through Pixel 10 devices with features including Bubbles floating-app windows, Screen Reactions, and security upgrades like temporary location permissions and capped PIN guessing. Gemini Intelligence ships later this summer to select advanced devices, and other OEMs including Samsung, with One UI 9, are expected to announce Android 17-based updates in the coming weeks.

title: Windows 11 KB5094126 BSODing, freezing, forcing BitLocker lockout, breaks OneDrive, and more
blurb: Microsoft's Windows 11 Patch Tuesday update KB5094126 is triggering blue screens, system freezes, BitLocker recovery lockouts, and broken OneDrive and Dropbox access, though the company has not yet acknowledged the problems. HP machines hitting the 0xc0430001 BSOD appear tied to Secure Boot certificate changes, requiring admins to verify the boot.stl file is present on installation media before deploying the dynamic updates.

title: Pentagon boasts of using AI to write reports mandated by Congress
blurb: The Pentagon's chief technology officer Emil Michael told a Hudson Institute event that the Department of Defense is using generative AI to draft its hundreds of congressionally mandated annual reports, cutting one report from 200 hours of staffing time to five. The tools run on GenAI.mil, the department's internal platform built on Google Cloud's Gemini for Government and available to all six military branches since December 2025.

title: Microsoft unveils new Surface Laptop with improved trackpad, Snapdragon X2, and more
blurb: Microsoft announced the eighth-generation Surface Laptop, powered by Snapdragon X2 Plus and X2 Elite chips with 80 TOPS NPUs for on-device AI, a haptic trackpad, up to 20 hours of battery, and a starting price of $1,599. The refresh holds the prior design but raises the entry price, pushing the mainstream Surface line further upmarket alongside the NVIDIA-equipped Surface Laptop Ultra.

title: Andera, which uses AI to automate corporate audit and compliance testing, raised a $37M Series A led by Lightspeed (Ryan Lawler/Axios)
blurb: Andera raised a $37M Series A led by Lightspeed to expand its platform that uses AI to automate corporate audit and compliance testing. The funding targets a control-testing workload that enterprises still handle largely through manual review and Big Four audit fees, positioning Andera against incumbent GRC vendors like AuditBoard and ServiceNow.

title: Okta expands Google Cloud partnership to secure AI agents and the browser
blurb: Okta expanded its Google Cloud partnership with integrations that bring identity governance to AI agents and tighten Chrome browser security, connecting Auth0 for AI Agents to Google's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and adding Device Assurance signals to the Chrome Device Trust Connector. The move lets enterprises register agents in a central directory, tie each to a human owner, and route requests through Okta, addressing the gap where only 34% of organizations apply human-grade controls to agents.

title: Who owns the control plane? Google Cloud Next 2026 and the real contest in agentic AI
blurb: The author argues the real enterprise AI contest has shifted from model selection to ownership of the agentic control plane, the semantic context layer that decides what agents know, what they can do, and who is accountable. The strategic warning is lock-in: Gartner expects 40% of enterprise apps to embed agents by end of 2026 but over 40% of agentic projects to be canceled by 2027, so enterprises that don't own their own ontology cede governance to whichever vendor controls that layer.

title: HPE product barrage targets AI networks, agents, management
blurb: HPE used its Discover event to launch a wide AI-infrastructure package, headlined by the Juniper QFX5140 switch for AI inferencing and edge deployments, a QFX5252 module for its 72-GPU AMD Helios training racks, and an AI-native SASE platform. The release deepens integration between HPE's acquired Juniper portfolio and its existing AI data center gear, positioning HPE against Cisco and Arista for enterprise AI networking buildouts.

title: Jamf announces new native AI Governance control plane for macOS
blurb: Jamf announced AI Governance, a capability in Jamf Pro that gives IT and security teams OS-level visibility and policy control over generative AI tools running on managed Macs, including local models, developer tools, and Model Context Protocol servers. The native approach closes a gap network proxies and cross-platform endpoint tools miss on Apple Silicon, where AI apps run as background processes, and arrives as Gartner projects AI governance spending to pass $1B by 2030.

title: Tether is shipping TurboQuant KV-cache quantization with Vulkan support into its QVAC SDK
blurb: Tether shipped TurboQuant KV-cache quantization with Vulkan support in version 0.12.0 of its QVAC SDK, cutting VRAM use by up to 5x with negligible precision loss regardless of context length. Tether is the first team to bring Google's KV-cache compression to a publicly available local model, making long-context inference viable on user-grade devices where an 8GB cache previously exceeded available memory.

title: Oracle’s Second Monthly Security Updates Deliver 245 Patches
blurb: Oracle released its June 2026 monthly Critical Security Patch Update with 245 new patches, roughly 120 rated critical and about 100 remotely exploitable without authentication, with Fusion Middleware accounting for more than 100 fixes. The advisory addresses PeopleSoft flaw CVE-2026-35273, which security firms tie to ShinyHunters attacks on at least 100 organizations, though Oracle stops short of confirming active exploitation.

title: How Siemens "slices the elephant," advancing agentic workflows for industrial software development
blurb: Siemens and Google Cloud built Knowledge Fabric, an agentic system that ingests Siemens' codebases of hundreds of millions of lines into knowledge graphs on Spanner Graph, paired with Gemini, the Agent Development Kit, Gemini CLI, and Anthropic's Claude Code. In a pilot migrating legacy frontends to web-based interfaces, the system cut implementation effort while preserving full system compatibility, signaling Google Cloud's push to win large enterprise modernization workloads.

title: UK.gov links up with LinkedIn for jobs market intel from 40M accounts
blurb: The UK government partnered with LinkedIn to tap labor-market data from 40M UK accounts, feeding real-time skills and hiring signals into its workforce and economic policy planning. The arrangement gives Microsoft's LinkedIn a public-sector data channel and raises questions about reliance on a single commercial platform for official jobs intelligence.

title: Microsoft sued over performance of Azure business
blurb: Microsoft is facing a shareholder class-action suit from a Michigan pension fund alleging it misled investors about Azure's slowing growth and the costs of its AI buildout between May 2025 and January 2026. The complaint, which names Satya Nadella and Amy Hood, ties the disclosures to the $357B single-day market-cap drop after Q2 results showed Azure growth easing to 39% with weaker guidance.

title: 5 things CIOs must do as sovereignty becomes a design constraint
blurb: Geographic location has shifted from a deployment detail to a core architectural constraint, as CIOs reweight sourcing decisions around geopolitical risk, AI regulation, and data residency rather than total cost of ownership. The piece argues enterprises aren't abandoning hyperscalers but are redesigning how they use them, treating sovereign cloud options and cross-border data flows as first-order design variables. The practical takeaway: build a regional regulatory map into architecture reviews before consolidating further on any single global platform.

title: Marvell details vision of optically-interconnected data centers spanning across thousands of kilometers — new interconnects sampling later this year would allow CSPs to pool resources based on workload
blurb: Marvell detailed plans for coherent optical interconnects that would let cloud providers pool compute and memory across data centers separated by thousands of kilometers, with its Colorz 1600 1.6 Tb/s solution sampling later this year. The technology targets hyperscalers whose AI workloads have outgrown single facilities, positioning Marvell's optics and Teralynx Ethernet switches as the layer that turns multi-site campuses into composable infrastructure.

title: Microsoft brings Planner Agent to all Microsoft 365 Copilot users
blurb: Microsoft made Planner Agent generally available to all Microsoft 365 Copilot license holders, letting them create, update, and prioritize tasks through natural language across Teams, Loop, and SharePoint. AI-generated plans land in draft mode for human approval before execution, and Microsoft routes complex requests like building plans from Office files to a more capable model while handling simple tasks with a lighter one.

title: Cyber threats stalking the 2026 World Cup
blurb: A Darktrace report released ahead of the 2026 World Cup found that 57% of professional sports organizations experienced multiple cyber incidents in the past 12 months, with 83% of their security staff saying they detected AI use in attacks against them. The average incident cost sports organizations roughly $169,000, a figure the firm expects to climb during the 48-team tournament as live-event timing widens the window for attackers.

title: NHS Palantir claims face scrutiny after data suggests uneven results
blurb: Reporting on NHS England's claims for Palantir's Federated Data Platform faces scrutiny after performance data suggests the rollout has delivered uneven results across trusts, with gains concentrated in a handful of sites rather than system-wide. The findings raise questions about the £330M contract's return as NHS leadership weighs further expansion, and they hand ammunition to critics already challenging Palantir's role in handling patient data.

title: Flash pricing — the elephant in the room for enterprise storage
blurb: The author argues that the decade-long vendor narrative of flash reaching pricing parity with HDDs was never true, and AI workloads have exposed it. Large AI datasets, object storage, and pre-compressed data deliver far lower data-reduction ratios than the "effective capacity" math assumed, while hyperscaler NAND buying squeezes SSD supply. Everpure's (formerly Pure Storage) CEO citing a third supply-chain disruption to justify price hikes signals enterprises should keep cheap HDDs in the mix rather than commit all-flash.

title: Project Jupiter AI data center build raises concerns about water usage in rural New Mexico desert — Oracle calls water usage 'negligible' for 11 million gallon one-time fill
blurb: Oracle's 1,400-acre Project Jupiter data center in New Mexico's Doña Ana County has drawn local opposition over water use, though Oracle calls its closed-loop cooling system "negligible" at an 11-million-gallon one-time fill plus 4,000 gallons annually for top-offs. The dispute lands in a desert region already ordered to cut groundwater use by 5.9 billion gallons per year under a Supreme Court Rio Grande ruling.

title: Microsoft's Copilot Cowork now generally available with usage-based billing
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide, adding usage-based billing through Copilot Credits priced at $0.01 each and metered on model use, context retrieval, tool calls, and runtime. The agentic feature runs long-running tasks on Anthropic's Opus 4.8 and Sonnet 4.6, requires a Microsoft 365 Copilot subscription, and ships with spending limits, alerts, and prepaid plans to give IT teams cost control.

title: Gemini in Chrome expands to more languages and regions, including Latin America, Africa, and the Middle East
blurb: Google expanded Gemini in Chrome to more languages and regions, including Latin America, Africa, and the Middle East, bringing tab summarization, content generation, and Gemini Live voice chat to all Workspace plans on ChromeOS, macOS, and Windows. The feature is on by default for eligible domains, so IT admins must opt out at the domain, OU, or group level if they want to gate browser-native AI access.

title: Tech debt, process gaps keep firms in AI ‘pilot purgatory,’ study finds
blurb: Tech debt and broken processes are stalling enterprise AI deployments in "pilot purgatory," with the world's top 2,000 public firms sitting on nearly $18 trillion in untapped AI value, according to Genpact research. The findings point at internal weaknesses rather than the technology itself, suggesting IT leaders need to fix process and infrastructure gaps before AI investments convert to returns.

title: Orca deploys quantum computer to major Japanese enterprise customer
blurb: Orca Computing deployed one of its PT-2 photonic quantum systems to an unnamed major Japanese industrial customer, marking the first commercial installation of its hardware in an enterprise facility rather than a research setting. Completed in under a week with partner Toyota Tsusho, the system integrates into the customer's existing cloud infrastructure for hybrid quantum-AI workloads and will later upgrade to Orca's PT-3 platform, expected to ship for benchmarking in August.

title: What’s new with Terraform + Ansible
blurb: HashiCorp released Terraform Ansible collection 2.0, powered by pyTFE, giving teams an API-first way to manage Terraform workspaces, runs, remote execution, and outputs directly from Ansible playbooks under role-based access controls. The collection eliminates the custom scripts and brittle glue code teams previously maintained to keep the two tools in sync, cutting maintenance overhead and reducing the upgrade breakage that disconnected permission models caused.

title: Amazon S3 annotations: attach rich, queryable context directly to your objects
blurb: AWS announced S3 annotations, letting teams attach up to 1,000 named annotations per object (1 MB each, 1 GB total) in JSON, XML, YAML, or plain text, editable without rewriting the object. Annotations travel with objects through copy, replication, and cross-region transfers, and flow into S3 Metadata tables queryable from Athena, giving agentic workflows scalable context without expensive retrieval.

title: Introducing tfctl: The CLI for HCP Terraform and TFE
blurb: HashiCorp released tfctl, the first official CLI for HCP Terraform and Terraform Enterprise platform operations like canceling runs, creating workspaces, and managing variables that the Terraform CLI doesn't cover. Built on the OpenAPI spec for full API coverage, it ships with dry-run guardrails, schema discovery, and JSON, markdown, or table output, and is scoped so delete commands require interactive confirmation to keep autonomous agents from running them.

title: Tesco is sprinting to quit VMware and Broadcom despite rapid migration risks
blurb: Tesco is accelerating its exit from VMware and Broadcom, moving production workloads off the stack faster than typical migration timelines to escape steep post-acquisition licensing costs. The compressed schedule raises blast-radius and rollback concerns, since rapid hypervisor migration at retail scale leaves little room to validate storage, networking, and failover before cutover.

title: Cloudflare DMARC Management is now generally available
blurb: Cloudflare made DMARC Management generally available to all customers for free, shipping a redesigned dashboard that gives a unified view of email authentication posture and guides domains toward full DMARC enforcement without parsing XML reports by hand. The tool ties together SPF, DKIM, and DMARC reporting to surface who is spoofing your domain and block impersonation before mail reaches inboxes.

title: HPE Discover: Neri outlines an AI architecture built for agents
blurb: At HPE Discover 2026, CEO Antonio Neri detailed an AI infrastructure stack rebuilt for agentic workloads, including Private Cloud AI scaling to 256 GPUs, the Alletra MPX 10,000 storage layer with native MCP support, and Juniper-based networking from GPU racks to the inference edge. The agentic operations tier adds zero-code agent registration across any framework, a three-tier identity model, and Zerto clean-state rollback for when agents err.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less (xpost)
blurb: Charity Majors argues that AI-assisted development raises the bar for engineering discipline rather than lowering it, drawing a parallel to the shift from hand-maintained server pets to immutable infrastructure. The piece contends that generating more code faster makes review, testing, observability, and ownership boundaries more important, since teams now ship volume they did not personally write and must still operate in production.

title: Report: Microsoft to use AWS to help GitHub deal with a major surge in demand
blurb: GitHub is tapping AWS for short-term capacity to absorb a 30x demand surge driven by agentic coding tools, after an October 2025 plan to scale 10x proved insufficient and triggered several small outages. The move is a stopgap multi-cloud play while Microsoft continues its longer migration to fully host GitHub on Azure by 2027.

title: 10Gb/s Ethernet: switching to a Broadcom SFP+ module
blurb: A 10GBASE-T SFP+ module in the author's study switch was overheating to 93C and thermal-flapping, shutting down at 95C and dropping the LAN until air conditioning kept it cool. The fix was swapping the Marvell-based MikroTik S+RJ10 for a Broadcom BCM84891-based 10Gtek module, which draws roughly 1.6W versus the older generation's higher power draw. The takeaway for anyone running 10GBASE-T over copper: check the PHY chip, since the Broadcom generation runs cool enough to avoid 24/7 cooling as a workaround.

title: Improving Arm64 support in CNCF projects with OCI credits
blurb: The CNCF detailed how it's using Oracle Cloud Infrastructure credits to expand Arm64 build and test coverage across its projects, addressing a gap as Arm instances now make up over 50% of new AWS launches and a third on Azure. The credits give maintainers native Arm64 CI runners, removing the emulation overhead and cross-compilation toil that previously slowed multi-arch release pipelines.

title: Why cloud native belongs at the heart of agentic AI: Lessons from building a multi-agent security platform on Kubernetes
blurb: An engineer at Orange Innovation documents building an internal real-time security-operations platform where each AI agent runs as its own Kubernetes Deployment, not an in-process module, giving every agent independent resource limits, identity, and restart policy. The stack pairs Falco with eBPF and an Isolation Forest model to pre-filter syscall events before they reach LangGraph-driven agents coordinated over A2A. Treating agents as microservices means canary rollouts, HPA, and namespace isolation apply without reinvention.

title: Intel-born networking tech resurfaces as InfiniBand alternative for DoE supers
blurb: Cornelis Networks is positioning Omni-Path, the high-speed interconnect Intel originally developed and spun out, as an Ethernet- and InfiniBand-competitive fabric for Department of Energy supercomputers chasing lower-latency collective operations at exascale. The pitch targets HPC and AI clusters where InfiniBand's Nvidia ownership and Ethernet's congestion behavior leave operators wanting a third option for predictable tail latency on tightly coupled jobs.

title: Brit competition cops order Google to make search rankings less mysterious
blurb: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority ordered Google to give businesses clearer information about how its search rankings work, using new powers that designate Google a holder of strategic market status. The ruling means SEO and content teams may finally get documented signals on what drives placement, reducing reliance on reverse-engineering and third-party guesswork to defend organic traffic.

title: Adobe brings GEO to CX Enterprise with Adobe Brand Visibility
blurb: Adobe launched Adobe Brand Visibility, a generative engine optimization product inside Adobe CX Enterprise that draws on nearly 300M real-world AI search prompts to show brands where they win or lose citations across ChatGPT, Google AI Mode, Microsoft Copilot, and Perplexity. The release, Adobe's first GEO tool since its May Semrush acquisition, lands as AI traffic to US retail sites jumped 1,324% between October 2024 and May 2026.

title: How Stripe, Google, Canva, Cloudflare and Higgsfield Are Actually Selling in 2026
blurb: Across six SaaStr AI 2026 GTM sessions, Stripe, Google, Canva, Cloudflare, Owner, and Higgsfield independently converged on the same four patterns: build and sell in parallel, centralize AI intelligence, treat agents as a real customer, and charge for outcomes rather than seats. Stripe's data shows top AI companies grew 120% in 2025 and 175% in 2026, with Lovable hitting $100M in revenue in eight months and Cursor reaching a $1B run rate in under two years. The shared takeaway for B2B founders is that outcome-based pricing and agent-aware GTM are becoming table stakes.

title: Pinterest launches an experimental AI shopping app called ‘Ask Pinterest’
blurb: Pinterest launched Ask Pinterest, a limited-access experimental app that turns its Taste Graph into a conversational shopping interface for multi-step queries like planning a dinner party or furnishing a room. The company also introduced a Pinterest MCP and new AI ad tools for advertisers, positioning itself against Google, ChatGPT, and Meta in agentic shopping while keeping discovery on its own first-party data rather than licensing it out.

title: TikTok Shop Bans AI Voices, Prerecorded Audio From Promotional Livestreams
blurb: TikTok Shop banned AI-generated voices, prerecorded audio, slideshows, and static-image content from promotional livestreams and shoppable videos, requiring all verbal communication to happen in real time and creators to appear on camera with the physical product. The rules land as eMarketer projects TikTok Shop will hit $23.4B in US sales in 2026, and as 31% of consumers say AI-generated marketing makes them trust a brand less versus 7% who trust it more.

title: Meta launches AI Mode in Facebook search to answer questions
blurb: Meta launched AI Mode in Facebook Search, which uses Meta AI to answer questions directly with responses grounded in public posts, Groups, and Reels rather than a standard list of results. Meta hasn't said how AI Mode selects which content surfaces or whether brands and creators can see when their posts are used, leaving discovery and attribution on Facebook an open question for marketers.

title: Topics matter for third-party authority signals
blurb: AI search engines rebuild their trusted-source set for every topic, so off-property authority building has to be topic-specific rather than generic digital PR. In one sample, competitor domains accounted for 33.5% of AI citations on invoicing queries but only 7% on starting-a-business queries, meaning the source types AI reaches for nearly flip between subjects. Audit the exact publishers, research, and expert sources AI cites for your target topic, then aim links and mentions there instead of borrowing a PR plan from a neighboring vertical.

title: X Enhances Ads Manager With Unified Conversion Tracking, Live Diagnostics
